In veluet! where could you ha' contented your ſelfe
VVith cheeſe, ſalt-butter, and a pickled hering,
I' the Low-countries; there worne cloth, and fuſtian!
Beene ſatisfied with a leape o' your Hoſt's daughter,
In garriſon, a wench of a ſtoter! or,
Your Sutlers wife, i' the leaguer, of two blanks!
You neuer, then, had runne vpon this flat,
To write your letters miſſiue, and ſend out
Your priuy ſeales, that thus haue frighted off
All your acquintance; that they ſhun you at diſtance,
VVorſe, then you do the Bailies!

sp.speaker: EV.
sp.l: Pox vpon you.
Hee repines, I come not to you for counſell, I lacke money.

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: You doe not thinke, what you owe me already?

sp.speaker: EV.
sp.l: I?
They owe you, that meane to pay you. I'll beſworne,
I neuer meant it. Come, you will proiect,
and threatens him. I ſhall vndoe your practice, for this moneth elſe:
You know mee.

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: I, yo' are a right ſweet nature!

sp.speaker: EV.
sp.l: Well, that's all one!

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: You'll leaue this Empire, one day?
You will not euer haue this tribute payd,
Your ſcepter o' the ſword?

sp.speaker: EV.
sp.l: Tye vp your wit,
Doe, and prouoke me not—

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: Will you, Sir, helpe,
To what I ſhall prouoke another for you?

sp.speaker: EV.
sp.l: I cannot tell; try me: I thinke I am not
So vtterly, of an ore vn-to-be-melted,
They ioyne. But I can doe my ſelfe good, on occaſions.

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: Strike in then, for your part. Mr. Fitz-dottrel
If I tranſgreſſe in point of manners, afford mee
Your beſt conſtruction; I muſt beg my freedome
Mere-craft pretends buſineſſe. From your affayres, this day.

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: How, Sr.

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: It is
In ſuccour of this Gentlemans occaſions,
My kinſ-man—

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: You'll not do me that affront, Sr.

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: I am ſory you ſhould ſo interpret it,
But, Sir, it ſtands vpon his being inueſted
In a new office, hee has ſtood for, long:Mere-craft deſcribes the office of Dependancy.
Maſter of the Dependances! A place
Or my proiection too, Sir, and hath met
Much oppoſition; but the State, now, ſee's
That great neceſſity of it, as after all
Their writing, and their ſpeaking, againſt Duells,
They haue erected it. His booke is drawne—
For, ſince, there will be differences, daily,
'Twixt Gentlemen; and that the roaring manner
Is growne offenſiue; that thoſe few, we call
The ciuill men o' the ſword, abhorre the vapours;
They ſhall refer now, hither, for their proceſſe;
And ſuch as treſpaſſe 'gainſt the rule of Court,
Are to be fin'd—

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: In troth, a pretty place!

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: A kinde of arbitrary Court 'twill be, Sir.

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: I ſhall haue matter for it, I beleeue,
Ere it be long: I had a diſtaſt.

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: But now, Sir,
My learned councell, they muſt haue a feeling,
They'll part, Sir, with no bookes, without the hand-gout
Be oyld, and I muſt furniſh. If 't be money,
To me ſtreight. I am Mine, Mint and Exchequer,
To ſupply all. What is't? a hundred pound?

sp.speaker: EVE.
sp.l: No, th' Harpey, now, ſtands on a hundred pieces.

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: Why, he muſt haue 'hem, if he will. To morrow, Sir,
Will equally ſerue your occaſion's,—
And therefore, let me obtaine, that you will yeeld
To timing a poore Gentlemans diſtreſſes,
In termes of hazard.—

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: By no meanes!

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: I muſt
Get him this money, and will.—

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: Sir, I proteſt,
I'd rather ſtand engag'd for it my ſelfe:
Then you ſhould leaue mee.

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: O good Sr. do you thinke
So courſely of our manners, that we would,
For any need of ours, be preſt to take it:
Though you be pleas'd to offer it.

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: Why, by heauen,
I meane it!

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: I can neuer beleeue leſſe.
But wee, Sir, muſt preſerue our dignity, Hee offers to be gone.
As you doe publiſh yours. By your faire leaue, Sir.

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: As I am a Gentleman, if you doe offer
To leaue mee now, or if you doe refuſe mee,
I will not thinke you loue mee.

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: Sir, I honour you.
And with iuſt reaſon, for theſe noble notes,
Of the nobility, you pretend too! But, Sir—
I would know, why? a motiue (he a ſtranger)
You ſhould doe this?

sp.speaker: (EVE.
sp.l: You'll mar all with your fineneſſe)

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: Why, that's all one, if 'twere, Sir, but my fancy.
But I haue a Buſineſſe, that perhaps I'd haue
Brought to his office.

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: O, Sir! I haue done, then;
If hee can be made profitable, to you.

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: Yes, and it ſhall be one of my ambitions
To haue it the firſt Buſineſſe? May I not?

sp.speaker: EVE.
sp.l: So you doe meane to make't, a perfect Buſineſſe.

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: Nay, I'll doe that, aſſure you: ſhew me once.

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: Sr, it concernes, the firſt be a perfect Buſineſſe,
For his owne honour!

sp.speaker: EVE.
sp.l: I, and th' reputation
Too, of my place.

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: Why, why doe I take this courſe, elſe?
I am not altogether, an Aſſe, good Gentlemen,
Wherefore ſhould I conſult you? doe you thinke?
To make a ſong on't? How's your manner? tell vs.

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: Doe, ſatisfie him: giue him the whole courſe.

sp.speaker: EVE.
sp.l: Firſt, by requeſt, or otherwiſe, you offer
Your Buſineſſe to the Court: wherein you craue:
The iudgement of the Maſter and the Aſsiſtants.

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: Well, that's done, now, what doe you vpon it?

sp.speaker: EVE.
sp.l: We ſtreight Sr, haue recourſe to the ſpring-head;
Viſit the ground; and, ſo diſcloſe the nature:
If it will carry, or no. If wee doe finde,
By our proportions it is like to proue
A ſullen, and blacke Bus'neſſe That it be
Incorrigible; and out of, treaty; then,
We file it, a Dependance!

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: So 'tis fil'd.
What followes? I doe loue the order of theſe things.

sp.speaker: EVE.
sp.l: We then aduiſe the party, if he be
A man of meanes, and hauings, that forth-with,
He ſettle his eſtate: if not, at leaſt
That he pretend it. For, by that, the world
Takes notice, that it now is a Dependance.
And this we call, Sir, Publication.

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: Very ſufficient! After Publication, now?

sp.speaker: EVE.
sp.l: Then we grant out our Proceſſe, which is diuers;
Eyther by Chartell, Sir, or ore-tenus,
Wherein the Challenger, and Challengee
Or (with your Spaniard) your Prouocador,
And Prouocado, haue their ſeuerall courſes—

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: I haue enough on't! for an hundred pieces?
Yes, for two hundred, vnder-write me, doe.
Your man will take my bond?

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: That he will, ſure,
But, theſe ſame Citizens, they are ſuch ſharks!
He whiſpers Fitz-dottrell aſide. There's an old debt of forty, I ga' my word
For one is runne away, to the Bermudas,
And he will hooke in that, or he wi' not doe.

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: Why, let him. That and the ring, and a hundred pieces,
Will all but make two hundred?

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: No, no more, Sir.
And then Guilt-head What ready Arithmetique you haue? doe you heare?
A pretty mornings worke for you, this? Do it,
You ſhall ha' twenty pound on't.

sp.speaker: GVI.
sp.l: Twenty pieces?

sp.speaker: (PLV.
sp.l: Good Father, do't)

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: You will hooke ſtill? well,
Shew vs your ring. You could not ha' done this, now
With gentleneſſe, at firſt, wee might ha' thank'd you?
But groane, and ha' you courteſies come from you
Like a hard ſtoole, and ſtinke? A man may draw
Your teeth out eaſier, then your money? Come, Hee pulls Plutarchus by the lips.
Were little Guilt-head heere, no better a nature,
I ſhould ne'r loue him, that could pull his lips off, now!
Was not thy mother a Gentlewoman?

sp.speaker: PLV.
sp.l: Yes, Sir.

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: And went to the Court at Chriſtmas, and St. Georges-tide?
And lent the Lords-men, chaines?

sp.speaker: PLV.
sp.l: Of gold, and pearle, Sr.

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: I knew, thou muſt take, after ſome body!
Thou could'ſt not be elſe. This was no ſhop-looke!
I'll ha' thee Captaine Guilt-head, and march vp,
And take in Pimlico,, and kill the buſh,
At euery tauerne! Thou ſhalt haue a wife,
If ſmocks will mount, boy. How now? you ha' there now
Some Briſto-ſtone, He turnes to old Guilt-head. or Corniſh counterfeit
You'ld put vpon vs.

sp.speaker: GVI.
sp.l: No, Sir, I aſſure you:
Looke on his luſter! hee will ſpeake himſelfe!
I'le gi' you leaue to put him i' the Mill,
H' is no great, large ſtone, but a true Paragon,
H' has all his corners, view him well.

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: H' is yellow.

sp.speaker: GVI.
sp.l: Vpo' my faith, Sr, o' the right black-water,
And very deepe! H' is ſet without a foyle, too.
Here's one o' the yellow-water, I'll ſell cheape.

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: And what do you valew this, at? thirty pound?

sp.speaker: GVI.
sp.l: No, Sir, he coſt me forty, ere he was ſet.

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: Turnings, you meane? I know your Equiuocks:
You' are growne the better Fathers of 'hem o' late.
Well, where't muſt goe, 't will be iudg'd, and, therefore,
Looke you't be right. You ſhall haue fifty pound for't. Now to Fitz-dottrel.
Not a deneer more! And, becauſe you would
Haue things diſpatch'd, Sir, I'll goe preſently,
Inquire out this Lady. If you thinke good, Sir.
Hauing an hundred pieces ready, you may
Part with thoſe, now, to ſerue my kinſmans turnes,
That he may wait vpon you, anon, the freer;
And take 'hem when you ha' ſeal'd, a gaine, of Guilt-head.

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: I care not if I do!

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: And diſpatch all,
Together.

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: There, th' are iuſt: a hundred pieces!
I' ha' told 'hem ouer, twice a day, theſe two moneths. Hee turnes 'hem out together. And Euerill and hee fall to ſhare.

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: Well, go, and ſeale then, Sr, make your returne
As ſpeedy as you can.

sp.speaker: EVE.
sp.l: Come gi' mee.

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: Soft, Sir,

sp.speaker: EVE.
sp.l: Mary, and faire too, then. I'll no delaying, Sir.

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: But, you will heare?

sp.speaker: EV.
sp.l: Yes, when I haue my diuident.

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: Theres forty pieces for you.

sp.speaker: EVE.
sp.l: What is this for?

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: Your halfe. You know, that Guilt-head muſt ha' twenty.

sp.speaker: EVE.
sp.l: And what's your ring there? ſhall I ha' none o' that?

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: O, thats to be giuen to a Lady!

sp.speaker: EVE.
sp.l: Is't ſo?

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: By that good light, it is.

sp.speaker: EV.
sp.l: Come, gi' me
Ten pieces more, then.

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: Why?

sp.speaker: EV.
sp.l: For Guilt-head? Sir,
Do' you thinke, I'll 'low him any ſuch ſhare:

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: You muſt.

sp.speaker: EVE.
sp.l: Muſt I? Doe you your muſts, Sir, I'll doe mine,
You wi'not part with the whole, Sir? Will you? Goe too.
Gi' me ten pieces!

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: By what law, doe you this?

sp.speaker: EVE.
sp.l: E'n Lyon-law, Sir, I muſt roare elſe.

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: Good!

sp.speaker: EVE.
sp.l: Yo' haue heard, how th' Aſſe made his diuiſions, wiſely?

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: And, I am he: I thanke you.

sp.speaker: EV.
sp.l: Much good do you, Sr.

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: I ſhall be rid o' this tyranny, one day?

sp.speaker: EVE.
sp.l: Not,
While you doe eate; and lie, about the towne, here;
And coozen i' your bullions; and I ſtand
Your name of credit, and compound your buſineſſe;
Adiourne your beatings euery terme; and make
New parties for your proiects. I haue, now,
A pretty taſque, of it, to hold you in
Wi' your Lady Tayle-buſh: but the toy will be,
How we ſhall both come off?

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: Leaue you your doubting.
And doe your portion, what's aſſign'd you: I
Neuer fail'd yet.

sp.speaker: EVE.
sp.l: With reference to your aydes?
You'll ſtill be vnthankfull. Where ſhall I meete you, anon?
You ha' ſome feate to doe alone, now, I ſee;
You wiſh me gone, well, I will finde you out,
And bring you after to the audit.

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: S'light!
There's Ingines ſhare too, I had forgot! This raigne
Is too-too-vnſuportable! I muſt
Quit my ſelfe of this vaſſalage! Ingine! welcome.
HOw goes the cry?

sp.speaker: ING.
sp.l: Excellent well!

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: Wil't do?
VVhere's Robinſon?

sp.speaker: ING.
sp.l: Here is the Gentleman, Sir.
VVill undertake t' himſelfe. I haue acquainted him,

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: VVhy did you ſo?

sp.speaker: ING.
sp.l: VVhy, Robinſon would ha' told him,
You know. And hee's a pleaſant wit! will hurt
Nothing you purpoſe. Then, he' is of opinion,
That Robinſon might want audacity,
She being ſuch a gallant. Now, hee has beene,
In Spaine, and knowes the faſhions there; and can
Diſcourſe; and being but mirth (hee ſaies) leaue much,
To his care:

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: But he is too tall!

sp.speaker: ING.
sp.l: For that, He excepts at his ſtature.
He has the braueſt deuice! (you'll loue him for't)
To ſay, he weares Cioppinos: and they doe ſo
In Spaine. And Robinſon's as tall, as hee.

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: Is he ſo?

sp.speaker: ING.
sp.l: Euery iot.

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: Nay, I had rather
To truſt a Gentleman with it, o' the two.

sp.speaker: ING.
sp.l: Pray you goe to him, then, Sir, and ſalute him.

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: Sir, my friend Ingine has acquainted you
With a ſtrange buſineſſe, here.

sp.speaker: WIT.
sp.l: A merry one, Sir.
The Duke of Drown'd-land, and his Dutcheſſe?

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: Yes, Sir.
Now, that the Coniurers ha' laid him by,
I ha' made bold, to borrow him a while;

sp.speaker: WIT.
sp.l: With purpoſe, yet, to put him out I hope
To his beſt vſe?

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: Yes, Sir.

sp.speaker: WIT.
sp.l: For that ſmall part,
That I am truſted with, put off your care:
I would not loſe to doe it, for the mirth,
Will follow of it; and well, I haue a fancy.

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: Sir, that will make it well.

sp.speaker: WIT.
sp.l: You will report it ſo.
Where muſt I haue my dreſſing?

sp.speaker: ING.
sp.l: At my houſe, Sir.

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: You ſhall haue caution, Sir, for what he yeelds,
To ſix pence.

sp.speaker: WIT.
sp.l: You ſhall pardon me. I will ſhare, Sir,
I' your ſports, onely: nothing i' your purchaſe.
But you muſt furniſh mee with complements,
To th' manner of Spaine; my coach, my guarda duenn'as;

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: Ingine's your Pro'uedor. But, Sir, I muſt
(Now I' haue entred truſt wi' you, thus farre)
Secure ſtill i' your quality, acquaint you
With ſomewhat, beyond this. The place, deſign'd
To be the Scene, for this our mery matter,
Becauſe it muſt haue countenance of women,
To draw diſcourſe, and offer it, is here by,
At the Lady Taile-buſhes.

sp.speaker: WIT.
sp.l: I know her, Sir,
And her Gentleman hutſher.

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: Mr Ambler?

sp.speaker: WIT.
sp.l: Yes, Sir.

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: Sir, It ſhall be no ſhame to mee, to confeſſe
To you, that wee poore Gentlemen, that want acres,
Muſt for our needs, turne fooles vp, and plough Ladies
Sometimes, to try what glebe they are: and this
Is no vnfruitefull piece. She, and I now,
Are on a proiect, for the fact, and venting
Of a new kinde of fucus (paint, for Ladies)
To ſerue the kingdome: wherein ſhee her ſelfe
Hath trauell'd, ſpecially, by way of ſeruice
Vnto her ſexe, and hopes to get the Monopoly,
As the reward, of her inuention.

sp.speaker: WIT.
sp.l: What is her end, in this?

sp.speaker: EV.
sp.l: Merely ambition,
Sir, to grow great, and court it with the ſecret:
Though ſhee pretend ſome other. For, ſhe's dealing,
Already, vpon caution for the ſhares,
And Mr. Ambler, is hee nam'd Examiner
For the ingredients; and the Regiſter
Of what is vented; and ſhall keepe the Office.
Now, if ſhee breake with you, of this (as I
Muſt make the leading thred to your acquaintance,
That, how experience gotten i' your being
Abroad, will helpe our buſineſſe) thinke of ſome
Pretty additions, but to keepe her floting:
It may be, ſhee will offer you a part,
Any ſtrange names of—

sp.speaker: WIT.
sp.l: Sr, I haue my' inſtructions.
Is it not high time to be making ready?

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: Yes, Sir,

sp.speaker: ING.
sp.l: The foole's in ſight, Dottrel.

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: Away, then.
REturn'd ſo ſoone?

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: Yes, here's the ring: I ha' ſeal'd.
But there's not ſo much gold in all the row, he ſaies—
Till 't come fro' the Mint. 'Tis tane vp for the gameſters.

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: There's a ſhop-ſhift! plague on 'hem.

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: He do's ſweare it.

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: He'll ſweare, and forſweare too, it is his trade,
You ſhould not haue left him.

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: S'lid, I can goe backe,
And beat him, yet.

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: No, now let him alone.

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: I was ſo earneſt, after the maine Buſineſſe,
To haue this ring, gone.

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: True, and 'tis time.
I' haue learn'd, Sir, ſin' you went, her Ladi-ſhip eats
With the Lady Tail-buſh, here, hard by.

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: I'the lane here?

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: Yes, if you had a ſeruant, now of preſence,
Well cloth'd, and of an aëry voluble tongue,
Neither too bigge, or little for his mouth,
That could deliuer your wiues complement;
To ſend along withall.

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: I haue one Sir,
A very handſome, gentleman-like-fellow,
That I doe meane to make my Dutcheſſe Vſher—
I entertain'd him, but this morning, too:
I'll call him to you. The worſt of him, is his name!

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: She'll take no note of that, but of his meſſage. Hee ſhewes him his Pug.

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: Diuell! How like you him, Sir. Pace, go a little.
Let's ſee you moue.

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: He'll ſerue, Sr, giue it him:
And let him goe along with mee, I'll helpe
To preſent him, and it.

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: Looke, you doe ſirah,
Diſcharge this well, as you expect your place.
Do' you heare, goe on, come off with all your honours. Giues him inſtructions.
I would faine ſee him, do it.

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: Truſt him, with it;

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: Remember kiſſing of your hand, and anſwering
With the French-time, in flexure of your body.
I could now ſo inſtruct him— and for his words—

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: I'll put them in his mouth.

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: O, but I haue 'hem
O' the very Academies.

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: Sir, you'll haue vſe for 'hem,
Anon, your ſelfe, I warrant you: after dinner,
When you are call'd.

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: S'light, that'll be iuſt play-time. He longs to ſee the play.
It cannot be, I muſt not loſe the play!

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: Sir, but you muſt, if ſhe appoint to ſit.
And, ſhee's preſident.

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: S'lid, it is the Diuell! Becauſe it is the Diuell.

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: And, 'twere his Damme too, you muſt now apply
Your ſelfe, Sir, to this, wholly; or loſe all.

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: If I could but ſee a piece—

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: Sr. Neuer think on't.

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: Come but to one act, and I did not care—
But to be ſeene to riſe, and goe away,
To vex the Players, and to puniſh their Poet—
Keepe him in awe!

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: But ſay, that he be one,
Wi' not be aw'd! but laugh at you. How then?

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: Then he ſhall pay for 'his dinner himſelfe.

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: Perhaps,
He would doe that twice, rather then thanke you.
Come, get the Diuell out of your head, my Lord,
(I'll call you ſo in priuate ſtill) and take
Your Lord-ſhip i' your minde. You were, ſweete Lord, He puts him in mind of his quarrell.
In talke to bring a Buſineſſe to the Office.

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: Yes.

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: Why ſhould not you, Sr, carry it o' your ſelfe,
Before the Office be vp? and ſhew the world,
You had no need of any mans direction;
In point, Sir, of ſufficiency. I ſpeake
Againſt a kinſman, but as one that tenders
Your graces good.

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: I thanke you; to proceed—

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: To Publications: ha' your Deed drawne preſently.
And leaue a blancke to put in your Feoffees
One, two, or more, as you ſee cauſe—

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: I thank you
Heartily, I doe thanke you. Not a word more,
I pray you, as you loue mee. Let mee alone. He is angry with himſelfe.
That I could not thinke o' this, as well, as hee?
O, I could beat my infinite blocke-head—!

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: Come, we muſt this way.

sp.speaker: PVG.
sp.l: How far is't.

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: Hard by here
Ouer the way. Now, to atchieue this ring,
He thinkes how to coozen the bearer, of the ring. From this ſame fellow, that is to aſſure it;
Before hee giue it. Though my Spaniſh Lady,
Be a young Gentleman of meanes, and ſcorne
To ſhare, as hee doth ſay, I doe not know
How ſuch a toy may tempt his Lady-ſhip:
And therefore, I thinke beſt, it be aſſur'd.

sp.speaker: PVG.
sp.l: Sir, be the Ladies braue, wee goe vnto?

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: O, yes.

sp.speaker: PVG.
sp.l: And ſhall I ſee 'hem, and ſpeake to 'hem?

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: Queſtions his man. What elſe? ha' you your falſe-beard about you? Traines.

sp.speaker: TRA.
sp.l: Yes,

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: And is this one of your double Cloakes?

sp.speaker: TRA.
sp.l: The beſt of 'hem.

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: Be ready then. Sweet Pitfall!
Offers to kiſſe. COme, I muſt buſſe—

sp.speaker: PIT.
sp.l: Away.

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: I'll ſet thee vp again.
Neuer feare that: canſt thou get ne'r a bird?
No Thruſhes hungry? Stay, till cold weather come,
I'll help thee to an Onſell, or, a Field-fare.
She runs in, in haſte: he followes. Who's within, with Madame?

sp.speaker: PIT.
sp.l: I'll tell you ſtraight.

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: Pleaſe you ſtay here, a while Sir, I'le goe in.

sp.speaker: PVG.
sp.l: I doe ſo long to haue a little venery,
Pug leaps at Pitfall's comming in. While I am in this body! I would taſt
Of euery ſinne, a little, if it might be
After the maner of man! Sweet-heart!

sp.speaker: PIT.
sp.l: What would you, Sr?

sp.speaker: PVG.
sp.l: Nothing but fall in, to you, be your Black-bird,
My pretty pit (as the Gentleman ſaid) your Throſtle:
Lye tame, and taken with you; here' is gold!
To buy you ſo much new ſtuffes, from the ſhop,
Traine's in his falſe cloak, brings a falſe meſſage, and gets the ring. Mere-craft followes preſently, and askes for it. Em. Train's as himſelfe againe. As I may take the old vp —

sp.speaker: TRA.
sp.l: You muſt ſend, Sir.
The Gentleman the ring.

sp.speaker: PVG.
sp.l: There 'tis. Nay looke,
Will you be fooliſh, Pit,

sp.speaker: PIT.
sp.l: This is ſtrange rudeneſſe.

sp.speaker: PVG.
sp.l: Deare Pit.

sp.speaker: PIT.
sp.l: I'll call, I ſweare.

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: Where are you, Sr?
Is your ring ready? Goe with me.

sp.speaker: PVG.
sp.l: I ſent it you.

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: Me? When? by whom?

sp.speaker: PVG.
sp.l: A fellow here, e'en now,
Came for it i' your name.

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: I ſent none, ſure.
My meaning euer was, you ſhould deliuer it,
Your ſelfe: So was your Maſters charge, you know.
What fellow was it, doe you know him?

sp.speaker: PVG.
sp.l: Here,
But now, he had it.

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: Saw you any? Traines?

sp.speaker: TRA.
sp.l: Not I.

sp.speaker: PVG.
sp.l: The Gentleman ſaw him.

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: Enquire.

sp.speaker: PVG.
sp.l: I was ſo earneſt vpon her, I mark'd not!The Diuell confeſſeth himſelfe coozen'd.
My diuelliſh Chiefe has put mee here in fleſh,
To ſhame mee! This dull body I am in,
I perceiue nothing with! I offer at nothing,
That will ſucceed!

sp.speaker: TRA.
sp.l: Sir, ſhe ſaw none, ſhe ſaies.

sp.speaker: PVG.
sp.l: Satan himſelfe, has tane a ſhape t' abuſe me. Mere-craft accuſeth him of negligence.
It could not be elſe!

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: This is aboue ſtrange!
That you ſhould be ſo retchleſſe. What'll you do, Sir?
How will you anſwer this, when you are queſtion'd?

sp.speaker: PVG.
sp.l: Run from my fleſh, if I could; put off mankind!
This's ſuch a ſcorne! and will be a new exerciſe,
For my Arch-Duke! Woe to the ſeuerall cudgells,
Muſt ſuffer, on this backe! Can you no ſuccours? Sir?He asketh ayde.

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: Alas! the vſe of it is ſo preſent.

sp.speaker: PVG.
sp.l: I aske,
Sir, credit for another, but till to morrow?

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: There is not ſo much time, Sir. But how euer,
The Lady is a noble Lady, and will
(To ſaue a Gentleman from check) be intreated
To ſay, ſhe ha's receiu'd it.

sp.speaker: PVG.
sp.l: Do you thinke ſo? Mere-craft promiſeth faintly, yet comforts him.
Will ſhee be won?

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: No doubt, to ſuch an office,
It will be a Lady's brauery, and her pride.

sp.speaker: PVG.
sp.l: And not be knowne on't after, vnto him?

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: That were a treachery! Vpon my word,
Be confident. Returne vnto your maſter,
My Lady Preſident ſits this after-noone,
Ha's tane the ring, commends her ſeruices
Vnto your Lady-Dutcheſſe. You may ſay
She's a ciuill Lady, and do's giue her
All her reſpects, already: Bad you, tell her
She liues, but to receiue her wiſh'd commandements,
And haue the honor here to kiſſe her hands:
For which ſhee'll ſtay this houre yet. Haſten you
Your Prince, away.

sp.speaker: PVG.
sp.l: And Sir, The Diuel is doubtfull. you will take care
Th' excuſe be perfect?

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: You confeſſe your feares.
Too much.

sp.speaker: PVG.
sp.l: The ſhame is more, I'll quit you of either.
A Pox vpo' referring to Commiſsioners,
I' had rather heare that it were paſt the ſeales:
Your Courtiers moue ſo Snaile-like i' your Buſineſſe.
Wuld I had not begun wi' you.

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: We muſt moue,
Madame, in order, by degrees: not iump.

sp.speaker: TAY.
sp.l: Why, there was Sr. Iohn Monie-man could iump
A Buſineſſe quickely.

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: True, hee had great friends,
But, becauſe ſome, ſweete Madame, can leape ditches,
Wee muſt not all ſhunne to goe ouer bridges.
The harder parts, I make account are done:
He flatters her. Now, 'tis referr'd. You are infinitly bound
Vnto the Ladies, they ha' ſo cri'd it vp!

sp.speaker: TAY.
sp.l: Doe they like it then?

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: They ha' ſent the Spaniſh-Lady,
To gratulate with you—

sp.speaker: TAY.
sp.l: I muſt ſend 'hem thankes
And ſome remembrances.

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: That you muſt, and viſit 'hem.
Where's Ambler?

sp.speaker: TAY.
sp.l: Loſt, to day, we cannot heare of him.

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: Not Madam?

sp.speaker: TAY.
sp.l: No in good faith. They ſay he lay not
At home, to night. And here has fall'n a Buſineſſe
Betweene your Couſin, and Maſter Manly, has
Vnquieted vs all.

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: So I heare, Madame.
Pray you how was it?

sp.speaker: TAY.
sp.l: Troth, it but appeares
Ill o' your Kinſmans part. You may haue heard,
That Manly is a ſutor to me, I doubt not:

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: I gueſs'd it, Madame.

sp.speaker: TAY.
sp.l: And it ſeemes, he truſted
Your Couſin to let fall ſome faire reports
Of him vnto mee.

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: Which he did!

sp.speaker: TAY.
sp.l: So farre
From it, as hee came in, and tooke him rayling
Againſt him.

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: How! And what ſaid Manly to him?

sp.speaker: TAY.
sp.l: Inough, I doe aſſure you: and with that ſcorne
Of him, and the iniury, as I doe wonder
How Euerill bore it! But that guilt vndoe's
Many mens valors

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: Here comes Manly.

sp.speaker: MAN.
sp.l: I'll take my leaue—

sp.speaker: TAY.
sp.l: You ſha' not goe, i' faith.
I'll ha' you ſtay, and ſee this Spaniſh miracle,
Of our Engliſh Ladie.

sp.speaker: MAN.
sp.l: Let me pray your Ladiſhip,
Lay your commands on me, ſome other time.

sp.speaker: TAY.
sp.l: Now, I proteſt: and I will haue all piec'd,
And friends againe.

sp.speaker: MAN.
sp.l: It will be but ill ſolder'd!

sp.speaker: TAY.
sp.l: You are too much affected with it.

sp.speaker: MAN.
sp.l: I cannot
Madame, but thinke on't for th' iniuſtice.

sp.speaker: TAY.
sp.l: Sir,
His kinſman here is ſorry.

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l:
Not I, Madam,
Mere-craft denies him.
I am no kin to him, wee but call Couſins,
And if wee were, Sir, I haue no relation
Vnto his crimes.

sp.speaker: MAN.
sp.l: You are not vrged with 'hem.
I can accuſe, Sir, none but mine owne iudgement,
For though it were his crime, ſo to betray mee:
I'am ſure, 'twas more mine owne, at all to truſt him.
But he, therein, did vſe but his old manners,
And ſauour ſtrongly what hee was before.

sp.speaker: TAY.
sp.l: Come, he will change!

sp.speaker: MAN.
sp.l: Faith, I muſt neuer think it.
Nor were it reaſon in mee to expect
That for my ſake, hee ſhould put off a nature
Hee ſuck'd in with his milke. It may be Madam,
Deceiuing truſt, is all he has to truſt to:
If ſo, I ſhall be loath, that any hope
Of mine, ſhould bate him of his meanes.

sp.speaker: TAY.
sp.l: Yo' are ſharp, Sir.
This act may make him honeſt!

sp.speaker: MAN
sp.l: If he were
To be made honeſt, by an act of Parliament,
I ſhould not alter, i' my faith of him.

sp.speaker: TAY.
sp.l: Welcome, deare Either-ſide! how haſt thou done, good wench?
Thou haſt beene a ſtranger! I ha' not ſeene thee, this weeke.
EVer your ſeruant, Madame.

sp.speaker: TAY.
sp.l: Where haſt 'hou beene?
I did ſo long to ſee thee.

sp.speaker: EIT.
sp.l: Viſiting, and ſo tyr'd!
I proteſt, Madame, 'tis a monſtrous trouble!

sp.speaker: TAY.
sp.l: And ſo it is. I ſweare I muſt to morrow,
Beginne my viſits (would they were ouer) at Court.
It tortures me, to thinke on 'hem.

sp.speaker: EIT.
sp.l: I doe heare
You ha' cauſe, Madam, your ſute goes on.

sp.speaker: TAY.
sp.l: Who told thee?

sp.speaker: EYT.
sp.l: One, that can tell: Mr. Eyther-ſide.

sp.speaker: TAY.
sp.l: O, thy huſband!
Yes faith, there's life in't, now: It is referr'd.
If wee once ſee it vnder the ſeales, wench, then,
Haue with 'hem for the great Carroch, ſixe horſes,
And the two Coach-men, with my Ambler, bare,
And my three women: wee will liue, i' faith,
The examples o' the towne, and gouerne it.
I'le lead the faſhion ſtill.

sp.speaker: EIT.
sp.l: You doe that, now,
Sweet Madame.

sp.speaker: TAY.
sp.l: O, but then, I'll euery day
Bring vp ſome new deuice. Thou and I, Either-ſide,
Will firſt be in it, I will giue it thee;
And they ſhall follow vs. Thou ſhalt, I ſweare,
Weare euery moneth a new gowne, out of it.

sp.speaker: EIT.
sp.l: Thanke you good Madame.

sp.speaker: TAY.
sp.l: Pray thee call mee Taile-buſh
As I thee, Either-ſide; I not loue this, Madame.

sp.speaker: EYT.
sp.l: Then I proteſt to you, Taile-buſh, I am glad
Your Buſineſſe ſo ſucceeds.

sp.speaker: TAY.
sp.l: Thanke thee, good Eyther-ſide.

sp.speaker: EYT.
sp.l: But Maſter Either-ſide tells me, that he likes
Your other Buſineſſe better.

sp.speaker: TAY.
sp.l: Which?

sp.speaker: EIT.
sp.l: O' the Tooth-picks.

sp.speaker: TAY.
sp.l: I neuer heard on't.

sp.speaker: EIT.
sp.l: Aske Mr. Mere-craft.

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: Madame? H'is one, in a word, I'll truſt his malice,
Mere-craft hath whiſper'd with the while. With any mans credit, I would haue abus'd!

sp.speaker: MAN.
sp.l: Sir, if you thinke you doe pleaſe mee, in this,
You are deceiu'd!

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: No, but becauſe my Lady,
Nam'd him my kinſman; I would ſatisfie you,
What I thinke of him: and pray you, vpon it
To iudge mee!

sp.speaker: MAN.
sp.l: So I doe: that ill mens friendſhip,
Is as vnfaithfull, as themſelues.

sp.speaker: TAY.
sp.l: Doe you heare?
Ha' you a Buſineſſe about Tooth-picks?

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: Yes, Madame.
Did I ne'r tell't you? I meant to haue offer'd it
Your Lady-ſhip, on the perfecting the pattent.

sp.speaker: TAY.
sp.l: How is't!

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: For ſeruing the whole ſtate with Tooth-picks; The Proiect for Tooth-picks.
Somewhat an intricate Buſineſſe to diſcourſe) but—
I ſhew, how much the Subiect is abus'd,
Firſt, in that one commodity? then what diſeaſes,
And putrefactions in the gummes are bred,
By thoſe are made' of' adultrate, and falſe wood?
My plot, for reformation of theſe, followes.
To haue all Tooth-picks, brought vnto an office,
There ſeal'd; and ſuch as counterfait 'hem, mulcted.
And laſt, for venting 'hem to haue a booke
Printed, to teach their vſe, which euery childe
Shall haue throughout the kingdome, that can read,
And learne to picke his teeth by. Which beginning
Barely to practice, with ſome other rules,
Of neuer ſleeping with the mouth open, Traines his man whiſpers him. chawing
Some graines of maſticke, will preſerue the breath
Pure, and ſo free from taym—ha' what is't? ſai'ſt thou?

sp.speaker: TAY.
sp.l: Good faith, it ſounds a very pretty Bus'neſſe!

sp.speaker: EIT.
sp.l: So Mr. Either-ſide ſaies, Madame.

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: The Lady is come.

sp.speaker: TAY.
sp.l: Is ſhe? Good, waite vpon her in. My Ambler
Was neuer ſo ill abſent. Either-ſide,
How doe I looke to day? Am I not dreſt, She lookes in her glaſſe
Spruntly?

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: Yes, verily, Madame.

sp.speaker: TAY.
sp.l: Pox o' Madame,
Will you not leaue that?

sp.speaker: EIT.
sp.l: Yes, good Taile-buſh.

sp.speaker: TAY.
sp.l: So?
Sounds not that better? What vile Fucus is this,
Thou haſt got on?

sp.speaker: EIT.
sp.l: 'Tis Pearle.

sp.speaker: TAY.
sp.l: As I breath, Either-ſide, I know't. Here comes
(They ſay) a wonder, ſirrah, has beene in Spaine!
Will teach vs all! ſhee's ſent to mee, from Court.
To gratulate with mee! Pr'y thee, let's obſerue her,
What faults ſhe has, that wee may laugh at 'hem,
When ſhe is gone,

sp.speaker: EIT.
sp.l: That we will heartily, Tail-buſh. Wittipol enters.

sp.speaker: TAY.
sp.l: O, mee! the very Infanta of the Giants!

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: Wittipol is dreſt like a Spaniſh Lady. Excuſes him ſelfe for not kiſsing. Here is a noble Lady, Madame, come,
From your great friends, at Court, to ſee your Ladi-ſhip:
And haue the honour of your acquaintance.

sp.speaker: TAY.
sp.l: Sir.
She do's vs honour.

sp.speaker: WIT.
sp.l: Pray you, ſay to her Ladiſhip,
It is the manner of Spaine, to imbrace onely,
Neuer to kiſſe. She will excuſe the cuſtome!

sp.speaker: TAY.
sp.l: Your vſe of it is law. Pleaſe you, ſweete, Madame,
To take a ſeate.

sp.speaker: WIT.
sp.l: Yes, Madame. I' haue had
The fauour, through a world of faire report
To know your vertues, Madame; and in that
Name, haue deſir'd the happineſſe of preſenting
My ſeruice to your Ladiſhip!

sp.speaker: TAY.
sp.l: Your loue, Madame,
I muſt not owne it elſe.

sp.speaker: WIT.
sp.l: Both are due, Madame,
To your great vndertakings.

sp.speaker: TAY.
sp.l: Great? In troth, Madame,
They are my friends, that thinke 'hem any thing:
If I can doe my ſexe (by 'hem) any ſeruice,
I' haue my ends, Madame.

sp.speaker: WIT.
sp.l: And they are noble ones,
That make a multitude beholden, Madame:
The common wealth of Ladies, muſt acknowledge from you.

sp.speaker: EIT.
sp.l: Except ſome enuious, Madame.

sp.speaker: WIT.
sp.l: Yo' are right in that, Madame,
Of which race, I encountred ſome but lately.
• ho ('t ſeemes) haue ſtudyed reaſons to diſcredit
Your buſineſſe.

sp.speaker: TAY.
sp.l: How, ſweet Madame.

sp.speaker: WIT.
sp.l: Nay, the parties
Wi' not be worth your pauſe— Moſt ruinous things, Madame,
That haue put off all hope of being recouer'd
To a degree of handſomeneſſe.

sp.speaker: TAY.
sp.l: But their reaſons, Madame?
I would faine heare.

sp.speaker: WIT.
sp.l: Some Madame, I remember.
They ſay, that painting quite deſtroyes the face—

sp.speaker: EIT.
sp.l: O, that's an old one, Madame.

sp.speaker: WIT.
sp.l: There are new ones, too.
Corrupts the breath; hath left ſo little ſweetneſſe
In kiſſing, as 'tis now vſ'd, but for faſhion:
And ſhortly will be taken for a puniſhment.
Decayes the fore-teeth, that ſhould guard the tongue;
And ſuffers that runne riot euer-laſting!
And (which is worſe) ſome Ladies when they meete
Manly begins to know him. Cannot be merry, and laugh, but they doe ſpit
In one anothers faces!

sp.speaker: MAN.
sp.l: I ſhould know
This voyce, and face too:

sp.speaker: WIT.
sp.l: Then they ſay, 'tis dangerous
To all the falne, yet well diſpos'd Mad-dames,
That are induſtrious, and deſire to earne
Their liuing with their ſweate! For any diſtemper
Of heat, and motion, may diſplace the colours;
And if the paint once runne about their faces,
Twenty to one, they will appeare ſo ill-fauour'd,
Their ſeruants run away, too, and leaue the pleaſure
Imperfect, and the reckoning alſ' vnpay'd.

sp.speaker: EIT.
sp.l: Pox, theſe are Poets reaſons.

sp.speaker: TAY.
sp.l: Some old Lady
That keepes a Poet, has deuis'd theſe ſcandales.

sp.speaker: EIT.
sp.l: Faith we muſt haue the Poets baniſh'd, Madame,
As Maſter Either-ſide ſaies.

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: Maſter Fitz dottrel?
And his wife: where? Madame, the Duke of Drown'd-land,
That will be ſhortly.

sp.speaker: VVIT.
sp.l: Is this my Lord?

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: The ſame.

div.head: ACT. IIIJ. SCENE. IV.
FITZ-DOTTREL. Miſtreſſe FITZ-DOTTRELL. PVG. to them.

sp.l: YOur ſeruant. Madame!

sp.speaker: VVIT.
sp.l: How now? Friend? offended, Wittipol whiſpers with Manly.
That I haue found your haunt here?

sp.speaker: MAN.
sp.l: No, but wondring
At your ſtrange faſhion'd venture, hither.

sp.speaker: VVIT.
sp.l: It is
To ſhew you what they are, you ſo purſue.

sp.speaker: MAN.
sp.l: I thinke't will proue a med'cine againſt marriage;
To know their manners.

sp.speaker: VVIT.
sp.l: Stay, and profit then.

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: The Lady, Madame, whoſe Prince has brought her, here, Hee preſents Miſtreſſe Fitz-dottrel.
To be inſtructed.

sp.speaker: VVIT.
sp.l: Pleaſe you ſit with vs, Lady.

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: That's Lady-Preſident.

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: A goodly woman!
I cannot ſee the ring, though.

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: Sir, ſhe has it.

sp.speaker: TAY.
sp.l: But, Madame, theſe are very feeble reaſons!

sp.speaker: WIT.
sp.l: So I vrg'd Madame, that the new complexion,
Now to come forth, in name o' your Ladiſhip's fucus,
Had no ingredient—

sp.speaker: TAY.
sp.l: But I durſt eate, I aſſure you.

sp.speaker: WIT.
sp.l: So do they, in Spaine.

sp.speaker: TAY.
sp.l: Sweet Madam be ſo liberall,
To giue vs ſome o' your Spaniſh Fucuſes!

sp.speaker: VVIT.
sp.l: They are infinit Madame.

sp.speaker: TAY.
sp.l: So I heare, they haue
VVater of Gourdes, of Radiſh, the white Beanes,
Flowers of Glaſſe, of Thiſtles, Roſe-marine.
Raw Honey, Muſtard-ſeed, and Bread dough-bak'd,
The crums o' bread, Goats-milke, and whites of Egges,
Campheere, and Lilly-roots, the fat of Swannes,
Marrow of Veale, white Pidgeons, and pine-kernells,
The ſeedes of Nettles, perse line, and hares gall.
Limons, thin-skind—

sp.speaker: EIT.
sp.l: How, her Ladiſhip has ſtudied
Al excellent things!

sp.speaker: VVIT.
sp.l: But ordinary, Madame.
No, the true rarities, are th' Aluagada,
And Argentata of Queene Iſabella!

sp.speaker: TAY.
sp.l: I, what are their ingredients, gentle Madame?

sp.speaker: WIT.
sp.l: Your Allum Scagliola, or Pol-dipedra;
And Zuccarino; Turpentine of Abezzo.
VVaſh'd in nine waters: Soda di leuante,
Or your Ferne aſhes; Beniamin di gotta;
Oyles of Lentiſco; Zucche Mugia; make
The admirable Verniſh for the face,
Giues the right luſter; but two drops tub'd on
VVith a piece of ſcarlet, makes a Lady of ſixty
Looke at ſixteen. But, aboue all, the water
Of the white Hen, of the Lady Eſtifanias!

sp.speaker: TAY.
sp.l: O, I, that ſame, good Madame, I haue heard of:
How is it done?

sp.speaker: VVIT.
sp.l: Madame, you take your Hen,
Plume it, and skin it, cleanſe it o' the inwards:
Then chop it, bones and all: adde to foure ounces
Of Carrnuacins, Pipitas, Sope of Cyprus,
Make the decoction, ſtreine it. Then diſtill it,
And keepe it in your galley-pot well, glidder'd:
Three drops preſerues from wrinkles, warts, ſpots, moles,
Blemiſh, or Sun-burnings, and keepes the skin
In decimo ſexto, euer bright, and ſmooth,
As any looking-glaſſe; and indeed, is call'd
The Virgins milke for the face, Oglio reale;
A Ceruſe, neyther cold or heat, will hurt;
And mixt with oyle of myrrhe, and the red Gilli-flower
Call'd Cataputia; and flowers of Rouiſtico;
Makes the beſt muta, or dye of the whole world.

sp.speaker: TAY.
sp.l: Deare Madame, will you let vs be familiar?

sp.speaker: WIT.
sp.l: Your Ladiſhips ſeruant.

sp.speaker: MER
sp.l: How do you like her.

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: Admirable!
Hee is iealous about his ring, and Mere-craft deliuers it. But, yet, I cannot ſee the ring.

sp.speaker: PVG.
sp.l: Sir.

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: I muſt
Deliuer it, or marre all. This foole's ſo iealous.
Madame— Sir, weare this ring, and pray you take knowledge,
'Twas ſent you by his wife. And giue her thanks,
Doe not you dwindle, Sir, beare vp.

sp.speaker: PVG.
sp.l: I thanke you, Sir,

sp.speaker: TAY.
sp.l: But for the manner of Spaine! Sweet, Madame, let vs
Be bold, now we are in: Are all the Ladies,
There, i' the faſhion?

sp.speaker: VVIT.
sp.l: None but Grandee's, Madame,
O' the claſp'd traine, which may be worne at length, too,
Or thus, vpon my arme.

sp.speaker: TAY.
sp.l: And doe they weare
Cioppino's all?

sp.speaker: VVIT.
sp.l: If they be dreſt in punto, Madame.

sp.speaker: EIT.
sp.l: Guilt as thoſe are? madame?

sp.speaker: WIT.
sp.l: Of Goldſmiths work, madame;
And ſet with diamants: and their Spaniſh pumps
Of perfum'd leather.

sp.speaker: TAI.
sp.l: I ſhould thinke it hard
To go in 'hem, madame.

sp.speaker: WIT.
sp.l: At the firſt, it is, madame.

sp.speaker: TAI.
sp.l: Do you neuer fall in 'hem?

sp.speaker: WIT.
sp.l: Neuer.

sp.speaker: EI.
sp.l: I ſweare, I ſhould
Six times an houre.

sp.speaker: WIT.
sp.l: But you haue men at hand, ſtill,
To helpe you, if you fall?

sp.speaker: EIT.
sp.l: Onely one, madame,
The Guardo-duennas, ſuch a little old man,
As this.

sp.speaker: EIT.
sp.l: Alas! hee can doe nothing! this!

sp.speaker: WIT.
sp.l: I'll tell you, madame, I ſaw i'the Court of Spaine once,
A Lady fall i'the Kings ſight, along.
And there ſhee lay, flat ſpred, as an Vmbrella,
Her hoope here crack'd; no man durſt reach a hand
To helpe her, till the Guarda-duenn'as came,
VVho is the perſon onel' allow'd to touch
A Lady there: and he but by this finger.

sp.speaker: EIT.
sp.l: Ha' they no ſeruants, madame, there? nor friends?

sp.speaker: WIT.
sp.l: An Eſcudero, or ſo madame, that wayts
Vpon 'hem in another Coach, at diſtance,
And when they walke, or daunce, holds by a hand-kercher,
Neuer preſumes to touch 'hem.

sp.speaker: EIT.
sp.l: This's ſciruy!
And a forc'd grauity! I doe not like it.
I like our owne much better.

sp.speaker: TAY.
sp.l: 'Tis more French,
And Courtly ours.

sp.speaker: EIT.
sp.l: And taſts more liberty.
VVe may haue our doozen of viſiters, at once,
Make loue t' vs.

sp.speaker: TAY.
sp.l: And before our husbands?

sp.speaker: EIT.
sp.l: Huſband?
As I am honeſt, Tayle-buſh I doe thinke
If no body ſhould loue mee, but my poore husband,
I ſhould e'n hang my ſelfe.

sp.speaker: TAY.
sp.l: Fortune forbid, wench:
So faire a necke ſhould haue ſo foule a neck-lace,

sp.speaker: EIT.
sp.l: 'Tis true, as I am handſome!

sp.speaker: WIT.
sp.l: I receiu'd, Lady,
A token from you, which I would not bee
Rude to refuſe, being your firſt remembrance.

sp.speaker: (FIT.
sp.l: O, I am ſatisfied now!

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: Do you ſee it, Sir.)

sp.speaker: WIT.
sp.l: But ſince you come, to know me, neerer, Lady,
I'll begge the honour, you will weare it for mee, Wittipol giues it Miſtreſſe Fitz-dottrel. Mere-craft murmures, He is ſatisfied, now he ſees it.
It muſt be ſo.

sp.speaker: Mrs. FIT.
sp.l: Sure I haue heard this tongue.

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: What do you meane, Sr?

sp.speaker: WIT.
sp.l: Would you ha'me mercenary?
We'll recompence it anon, in ſomewhat elſe,

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: I doe not loue to be gull'd, though in a toy.
VVife, doe you heare? yo' are come into the Schole, wife,
VVhere you may learne, I doe perceiue it, any thing!
How to be fine, or faire, or great, or proud,
Or what you will, indeed, wife; heere 'tis taught.
And I am glad on't, that you may not ſay,
Another day, when honours come vpon you,
You wanted meanes. I ha' done my parts: beene,
He vpbraids her, with his Bill of costs. To day, at fifty pound charge, firſt, for a ring,
To get you entred. Then left my new Play,
To wait vpon you, here, to ſee't confirm'd.
That I may ſay, both to mine owne eyes, and eares,
Senſes, you are my witneſſe, ſha' hath inioy'd
All helps that could be had, for loue, or money—

sp.speaker: Mrs. FIT.
sp.l: To make a foole of her.

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: Wife, that's your malice,
The wickedneſſe o' you nature to interpret
Your husbands kindeſſe thus. But I'll not leaue;
Still to doe good, for your deprau'd affections:
Intend it. Bend this ſtubborne will; be great.

sp.speaker: TAY.
sp.l: Good Madame, whom do they vſe in meſſages?

sp.speaker: WI.
sp.l: They cōmonly vſe their ſlaues, Madame.

sp.speaker: TAI.
sp.l: And do's your Ladiſhip.
Thinke that ſo good, Madame?

sp.speaker: WIT.
sp.l: no, indeed, Madame; I,
Therein preferre the faſhion of England farre,
Of your young delicate Page, or diſcreet Vſher,

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: And I goe with your Ladiſhip, in opinion,
Directly for your Gentleman-vſher,
There's not a finer Officer goes on ground.

sp.speaker: WIT.
sp.l: If hee be made and broken to his place, once.

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: Nay, ſo I preſuppoſe him.

sp.speaker: WIT.
sp.l: And they are fitter
Managers too, Sir, but I would haue 'hem call'd
Our Eſcudero's.

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: Good.

sp.speaker: WIT.
sp.l: Say, I ſhould ſend
To your Ladiſhip, who (I preſume) has gather'd
All the deare ſecrets, to know how to make
Paſtillos of the Dutcheſſe of Braganza,
Alcoreas, Muſtaccioli; or ſay it were
The Peladore of Iſabella, or balls
Againſt the itch, or aqua nanfa, or oyle
Of Ieſſamine for gloues, of the Marqueſſe Muja;
Or for the head, and hayre: why, theſe are offices

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: Fit for a gentleman, not a ſlaue. They onely
Might aske for your piueti, Spaniſh-cole,
To burne, and ſweeten a roome: but the Arcana
Of Ladies Cabinets—

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: Should be elſe-where truſted.
He enters himſelfe with the Ladie's Yo' are much about the truth. Sweet honoured Ladies,
Let mee fall in wi'you. I'ha' my female wit,
As well as my male. And I doe know what ſutes
A Lady of ſpirit, or a woman of faſhion!

sp.speaker: WIT.
sp.l: And you would haue your wife ſuch.

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: Yes, Madame, aërie,
Light; not to plaine diſhoneſty, I meane:
But, ſomewhat o'this ſide.

sp.speaker: WIT.
sp.l: I take you, Sir.
H' has reaſon Ladies. I'll not giue this ruſh
For any Lady, that cannot be honeſt
Within a thred.

sp.speaker: TAY.
sp.l: Yes, Madame, and yet venter
As far for th'other, in her Fame—

sp.speaker: WIT.
sp.l: As can be;
Coach it to Pimlico; daunce the Saraband;
Heare, and talke bawdy; laugh as loud, as a larum;
Squeake, ſpring, do any thing.

sp.speaker: EIT.
sp.l: In young company, Madame.

sp.speaker: TAY.
sp.l: Or afore gallants. If they be braue, or Lords,
A woman is ingag'd.

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: I ſay ſo, Ladies,
It is ciuility to deny vs nothing.

sp.speaker: PVG.
sp.l: You talke of a Vniuerſity! why, The Diuell admires him. Hell is
A Grammar-ſchoole to this!

sp.speaker: EIT.
sp.l: But then,
Shee muſt not loſe a looke on ſtuffes, or cloth, Madame.

sp.speaker: TAY.
sp.l: Nor no courſe fellow.

sp.speaker: WIT.
sp.l: She muſt be guided, Madame
By the clothes he weares, and company he is in;
Whom to ſalute, how farre—

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: I ha' told her this.
And how that bawdry too, vpo' the point,
Is (in it ſelfe) as ciuill a diſcourſe—

sp.speaker: WIT.
sp.l: As any other affayre of fleſh, what euer.

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: But ſhee will ne'r be capable, ſhee is not
So much as comming, Madame; I know not how
She loſes all her opportunities
With hoping to be forc'd. I' haue entertain'd
A gentleman, a younger brother, here, He ſhews his Pug.
Whom I would faine breed vp, her Eſcudero,
Againſt ſome expectation's that I haue,
And ſhe'll not countenance him.

sp.speaker: WIT.
sp.l: What's his name?

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: Diuel, o' Darbi-ſhire.

sp.speaker: EIT.
sp.l: Bleſſe vs from him!

sp.speaker: TAY.
sp.l: Call him De-uile, ſweet Madame.

sp.speaker: Mrs. FI.
sp.l: What you pleaſe, Ladies.

sp.speaker: TAY.
sp.l: De-uile's a prettier name!

sp.speaker: EIT.
sp.l: And ſounds, me thinks,
As it came in with the Conquerour—

sp.speaker: MAN.
sp.l: Ouer ſmocks!
What things they are? Manly goes out with indignation. That nature ſhould be at leaſure
Euer to make 'hem! my woing is at an end.

sp.speaker: WIT.
sp.l: What can he do?

sp.speaker: EIT.
sp.l: Let's heare him.

sp.speaker: TAY.
sp.l: Can he manage?

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: Pleaſe you to try him, Ladies. Stand forth, Diuell.

sp.speaker: PVG.
sp.l: Was all this but the preface to my torment?

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: Come, let their Ladiſhips ſee your honours.

sp.speaker: EIT.
sp.l: O,
Hee makes a wicked leg.

sp.speaker: TAY.
sp.l: As euer I ſaw!

sp.speaker: WIT.
sp.l: Fit for a Diuell.

sp.speaker: TAY.
sp.l: Good Madame, call him De-uile.

sp.speaker: WIT.
sp.l: De-uile, They begin their Catechiſme. what property is there moſt required
I' your conceit, now, in the Eſcudero?

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: Why doe you not ſpeake?

sp.speaker: PVG.
sp.l: A ſetled diſcreet paſe, Madame.

sp.speaker: WIT.
sp.l: I thinke, a barren head, Sir, Mountaine-like,
To be expos'd to the cruelty of weathers—

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: I, for his Valley is beneath the waſte, Madame,
And to be fruitfull there, it is ſufficient.
Dulneſſe vpon you! Could not you hit this?He ſtrikes him.

sp.speaker: PVG.
sp.l: Good Sir—

sp.speaker: WIT.
sp.l: He then had had no barren head.
You daw him too much, in troth, Sir.

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: I muſt walke
With the French ſticke, like an old vierger for you, The Diuell prayes.

sp.speaker: PVG.
sp.l: O, Chiefe, call mee to Hell againe, and free mee.

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: Do you murmur now?

sp.speaker: PVG.
sp.l: Not I, Sr.

sp.speaker: WIT.
sp.l: What do you take
Mr. Deuile, the height of your employment,
In the true perfect Eſcudero?

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: When?
What doe you anſwer?

sp.speaker: PVG.
sp.l: To be able, Madame,
Firſt to enquire, then report the working,
Of any Ladies phyſicke, in ſweete phraſe,

sp.speaker: WIT.
sp.l: Yes, that's an act of elegance, and importance.
But what aboue?

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: O, that I had a goad for him.

sp.speaker: PVG.
sp.l: To find out a good Corne-cutter.

sp.speaker: TAY.
sp.l: Out on him!

sp.speaker: EIT.
sp.l: Moſt barbarous!

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: Why did you doe this, now?
Of purpoſe to diſcredit me? you damn'd Diuell.

sp.speaker: PVG.
sp.l: Sure, if I be not yet, I ſhall be. All
My daies in Hell, were holy-daies to this!

sp.speaker: TAY.
sp.l: 'Tis labour loſt, Madame?

sp.speaker: EIT.
sp.l: H' is a dull fellow
Of no capacity!

sp.speaker: TAI.
sp.l: Of no diſcourſe!
O, if my Ambler had beene here!

sp.speaker: EIT.
sp.l: I, Madame;
You talke of a man, where is there ſuch another?

sp.speaker: WIT.
sp.l: Mr. Deuile, put caſe, one of my Ladies, heere,
Had a fine brach: and would imploy you forth
To treate 'bout a conuenient match for her.
What would you obſerue?

sp.speaker: PVG.
sp.l: The color, and the ſize, Madame.

sp.speaker: WIT.
sp.l: And nothing elſe?

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: The Moon, you calfe, the Moone!

sp.speaker: WIT.
sp.l: I, and the Signe.

sp.speaker: TAI.
sp.l: Yes, and receits for proneneſſe.

sp.speaker: WIT.
sp.l: Then when the Puppies came, what would you doe?

sp.speaker: PVG.
sp.l: Get their natiuities caſt!

sp.speaker: WIT.
sp.l: This's wel. What more?

sp.speaker: PVG.
sp.l: Conſult the Almanack-man which would be leaſt?
Which cleanelieſt?

sp.speaker: WIT.
sp.l: And which ſilenteſt? This's wel, madame!

sp.speaker: WIT.
sp.l: And while ſhe were with puppy?

sp.speaker: PVG.
sp.l: Walke her out,
And ayre her euery morning!

sp.speaker: WIT.
sp.l: Very good!
And be induſtrious to kill her fleas?

sp.speaker: PVG.
sp.l: Yes!

sp.speaker: WIT.
sp.l: He will make a pretty proficient.

sp.speaker: PVG.
sp.l: Who,
Comming from Hell, could looke for ſuch Catechiſing?
The Diuell is an Aſſe. I doe acknowledge it.

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: Fitz-dottrel admires Wittipol. The top of woman! All her ſexe in abſtract!
I loue her, to each ſyllable, falls from her.

sp.speaker: TAI.
sp.l: Good madame giue me leaue to goe aſide with him!
And try him a little!

sp.speaker: WIT.
sp.l: Do, and I'll with-draw, Madame,
The Diuell praies again. VVith this faire Lady: read to her, the while.

sp.speaker: TAI.
sp.l: Come, Sr.

sp.speaker: PVG.
sp.l: Deare Chiefe, relieue me, or I periſh.

sp.speaker: WIT.
sp.l: Lady, we'll follow. You are not iealous Sir?

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: He giues his wife to him, taking him to be a Lady. O, madame! you ſhall ſee. Stay wife, behold,
I giue her vp heere, abſolutely, to you,
She is your owne. Do with her what you will!
Melt, caſt, and forme her as you ſhall thinke good!
Set any ſtamp on! I'll receiue her from you
As a new thing, by your owne ſtandard!

sp.speaker: VVIT.
sp.l: Well, Sir!
BVt what ha' you done i' your Dependance, ſince?

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: O, it goes on, I met your Couſin, the Maſter—

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: You did not acquaint him, Sr?

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: Faith, but I did, Sr.
And vpon better thought, not without reaſon!
He being chiefe Officer, might ha' tane it ill, elſe,
As a Contempt againſt his Place, and that
In time Sir, ha' drawne on another Dependance.
No, I did finde him in good termes, and ready
To doe me any ſeruice.

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: So he ſaid, to you?
But Sr, you do not know him.

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: VVhy, I preſum'd
Becauſe this bus'neſſe of my wiues, requir'd mee,
I could not ha' done better: And hee told
Me, that he would goe preſently to your Councell,
A Knight, here, i' the Lane—

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: Yes, Iuſtice Either-ſide.

sp.speaker: FIT:
sp.l: And get the Feoffment drawne, with a letter of Atturney,
For liuerie and ſeiſen!

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: That I knowe's the courſe.
But Sir, you meane not to make him Feoffee?

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: Nay, that I'll pauſe on!

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: How now little Pit-fall.

sp.speaker: PIT.
sp.l: Your Couſin Maſter Euer-ill, would come in—
But he would know if Maſter Manly were heere. Mere-craft whiſpers againſt him.

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: No, tell him, if he were, I ha' made his peace!
Hee's one, Sir, has no State, and a man knowes not,
How ſuch a truſt may tempt him.

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: I conceiue you.

sp.speaker: EVE.
sp.l: Sr. this ſame deed is done here.

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: Pretty Plutarchus?
Art thou come with it? and has Sir Paul view'd it?

sp.speaker: PLV.
sp.l: His hand is to the draught.

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: VVill you ſtep in, Sr.
And read it?

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: Yes.

sp.speaker: EVE.
sp.l: I pray you a word wi' you. Eueril whiſpers againſt Mere-craft.
Sir Paul Eitherſide will'd mee gi' you caution,
VVhom you did make Feoffee: for 'tis the truſt
O' your whole State: and though my Couſin heere
Be a worthy Gentleman, yet his valour has
At the tall board bin queſtion'd; and we hold
Any man ſo impeach'd, of doubtfull honeſty!
I will not iuſtifie this; but giue it you
To make your profit of it: if you vtter it,
I can forſweare it!

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: I beleeue you, and thanke you, Sir.
BE not afraid, ſweet Lady: yo' are truſted
To loue, not violence here; I am no rauiſher,
But one, whom you, by your faire truſt againe,
May of a ſeruant make a moſt true friend.

sp.speaker: Mrs. FI.
sp.l: And ſuch a one I need, but not this way:
Sir, I confeſſe me to you, the meere manner
Of your attempting mee, this morning tooke mee,
And I did hold m'inuention, and my manners,
Were both engag'd, to giue it a requitall;
But not vnto your ends: my hope was then,
(Though interrrupted, ere it could be vtter'd)
That whom I found the Maſter of ſuch language,
That braine and ſpirit, for ſuch an enterpriſe,
Could not, but if thoſe ſuccours were demanded
To a right vſe, employ them vertuouſly!
And make that profit of his noble parts,
Which they would yeeld. Sr, you haue now the ground,
To exerciſe them in: I am a woman;
That cannot ſpeake more wretchedneſſe of my ſelfe,
Then you can read; match'd to a maſſe of folly;
That euery day makes haſte to his owne ruine;
The wealthy portion, that I brought him, ſpent;
And (through my friends neglect) no ioynture made me.
My fortunes ſtanding in this precipice,
'Tis Counſell that I want, and honeſt aides:
And in this name, I need you, for a friend!
Neuer in any other; for his ill,
Muſt not make me, Sr, worſe.

sp.speaker: MAN.
sp.l: O friend! forſake not
Manly, conceal'd this while ſhews himſelfe. The braue occaſion, vertue offers you,
To keepe you innocent: I haue fear'd for both;
And watch'd you, to preuent the ill I fear'd.
But, ſince the weaker ſide hath ſo aſſur'd mee,
Let not the ſtronger fall by his owne vice,
Or be the leſſe a friend, cauſe vertue needs him.

sp.speaker: WIT.
sp.l: Vertue ſhall neuer aske my ſuccours twice;
Moſt friend, moſt man; your Counſells are commands:
Lady, I can loue goodnes in you, more
Then I did Beauty; and doe here intitle
Your vertue, to the power, vpon a life
You ſhall engage in any fruitfull ſeruice,
Euen to forfeit.

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: Madame: Do you heare, Sir, Mere-craft takes Wittipol aſide, & moues a proiect for himſelfe.
We haue another leg-ſtrain'd, for this Dottrel.
He' ha's a quarrell to carry, and ha's cauſ'd
A deed of Feoffment, of his whole eſtate
To be drawne yonder; h' ha'ſt within: And you,
Onely, he meanes to make Feoffee. H'is falne
So deſperatly enamour'd on you, and talkes
Moſt like a mad-man: you did neuer heare
A Phrentick, ſo in loue with his owne fauour!
Now, you doe know, 'tis of no validity
In your name, as you ſtand; Therefore aduiſe him
To put in me. (h'is come here:) You ſhall ſhare Sir.

div.head: ACT. IV. SCENE. VIJ.
VVITTIPOL. Miſtreſſe FITZ-DOTTREL. MANLY. MERE-CRAFT. FITZ-DOTTRELL. EVERILL. PLVTARCHVS.

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: Madame, I haue a ſuit to you; and afore-hand,
I doe beſpeake you; you muſt not deny me,
I will be graunted.

sp.speaker: WIT.
sp.l: Sir, I muſt know it, though.

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: No Lady; you muſt not know it: yet, you muſt too.
For the truſt of it, and the fame indeed,
Which elſe were loſt me. I would vſe your name,
But in a Feoffment: make my whole eſtate
Ouer vnto you: a trifle, a thing of nothing,
Some eighteene hundred.

sp.speaker: WIT.
sp.l: Alas! I vnderſtand not
Thoſe things Sir. I am a woman, and moſt loath,
To embarque my ſelfe—

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: You will not ſlight me, Madame?

sp.speaker: WIT.
sp.l: Nor you'll not quarrell me?

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: No, ſweet Madame, I haue
Already a dependance; for which cauſe
I doe this: let me put you in, deare Madame, Hee hopes to be the man.
I may be fairely kill'd.

sp.speaker: WIT.
sp.l: You haue your friends, Sir,
About you here, for choice.

sp.speaker: EVE.
sp.l: She tells you right, Sir.

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: Death, if ſhe doe, what do I care for that?
Say, I would haue her tell me wrong.

sp.speaker: WIT.
sp.l: Why, Sir,
If for the truſt, you'll let me haue the honor
To name you one.

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: Nay, you do me the honor, Madame:
Sh • deſignes Manly. Who is't?

sp.speaker: WIT.
sp.l: This Gentleman:

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: O, no, ſweet Madame,
I • 'is friend to him, with whom I ha' the dependance.

sp.speaker: WIT.
sp.l: Who might he bee?

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: One Wittipol: do you know him?

sp.speaker: WIT.
sp.l: Alas Sir, he, a toy: This Gentleman
A friend to him? no more then I am Sir!

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: But will your Ladyſhip vndertake that, Madame?

sp.speaker: WIT.
sp.l: Yes, and what elſe, for him, you will engage me.

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: What is his name?

sp.speaker: VVIT.
sp.l: His name is Euſtace Manly.

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: VVhence do's he write himſelfe?

sp.speaker: VVIT.
sp.l: of Middle-ſex,

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: Say nothing, Madame. Clerke, come hether
VVrite Euſtace Manly, Squire o' Middle-ſex.

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: What ha' you done, Sir?

sp.speaker: VVIT.
sp.l: Nam'd a gentleman,
That I'll be anſwerable for, to you, Sir.
Had I nam'd you, it might ha' beene ſuſpected:
This way, 'tis ſafe.

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: Come Gentlemen, your hands,
Eueril applaudes it. For witnes.

sp.speaker: MAN.
sp.l: VVhat is this?

sp.speaker: EVE.
sp.l: You ha' made Election
Of a moſt worthy Gentleman!

sp.speaker: MAN.
sp.l: VVould one of worth
Had ſpoke it: whence it comes, it is
Rather a ſhame to me, then a praiſe.

sp.speaker: EVE.
sp.l: Sir, I will giue you any Satisfaction.

sp.speaker: MAN.
sp.l: Be ſilent then: "falſhood commends not truth.

sp.speaker: PLV.
sp.l: You do deliuer this, Sir, as your deed.
To th' vſe of Mr. Manly?

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: Yes: and Sir—
VVhen did you ſee yong Wittipol? I am ready,
For proceſſe now; Sir, this is Publication.
He ſhall heare from me, he would needes be courting
My wife, Sir.

sp.speaker: MAN.
sp.l: Yes: So witneſſeth his Cloake there.

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l:
Fitz-dottrel
• ſuſpicious of Manly ſtill. Nay good Sir, —Madame, you did vndertake—

sp.speaker: VVIT.
sp.l: VVhat?

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: That he was not Wittipols friend.

sp.speaker: VVIT.
sp.l: I heare
Sr. no confeſſion of it.

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: O ſhe know's not;
Now I remember, Madame! This young Wittipol,
VVould ha'd debauch'd my wife, and made me Cuckold,
Through a caſement; he did fly her home
To mine owne window: but I think I ſou't him,
And rauiſh'd her away, out of his pownces.
Tha' ſworne to ha' him by the eares: I feare
The toy, wi' not do me right.

sp.speaker: VVIT.
sp.l: No? that were pitty!
VVhat right doe you aske, Sir? Here he is will do't you?

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: Wittipol diſcouers himſelfe. Ha? Wittipol?

sp.speaker: VVIT.
sp.l: I Sir, no more Lady now,
Nor Spaniard!

sp.speaker: MAN.
sp.l: No indeed, 'tis Wittipol.

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: Am I the thing I fear'd?

sp.speaker: VVIT.
sp.l: A Cuckold? No Sir,
But you were late in poſſibility,
I'll tell you ſo much.

sp.speaker: MAN.
sp.l: But your wife's too vertuous!

sp.speaker: VVIT.
sp.l: VVee'll ſee her Sir, at home, and leaue you here,
To be made Duke o' Shore-ditch with a proiect.

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: Theeues, rauiſhers.

sp.speaker: VVIT.
sp.l: Crie but another note, Sir,
I'll marre the tune, o' your pipe!

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: Gi' me my deed, then. He would haue his deed again.

sp.speaker: VVIT.
sp.l: Neither: that ſhall be kept for your wiues good,
VVho will know, better how to vſe it.

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: Ha'
To feaſt you with my land?

sp.speaker: VVIT.
sp.l: Sir, be you quiet,
Or I ſhall gag you, ere I goe, conſult
Your Maſter of dependances; how to make this
A ſecond buſineſſe, you haue time Sir.

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: Oh! VVitipol bufflees him, and goes out
VVhat will the ghoſt of my wife Grandfather,
My learned Father, with my worſhipfull Mother,
Thinke of me now, that left me in this world
In ſtate to be their Heire? that am become
A Cuckold, and an Aſſe, and my wiues Ward;
Likely to looſe my land; ha' my throat cut:
All, by her practice!

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: Sir, we are all abus'd!

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: And be ſo ſtill! VVho hinders you, I pray you,
Let me alone, I would enioy my ſelfe,
And be the Duke o' Drown'd-Land, you ha' made me.

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: Sir, we muſt play an after-game o' this

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: But I am not in caſe to be a Gam-ſter:
I tell you once againe—

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: You muſt be rul'd
And take ſome counſell.

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: Sir, I do hate counſell,
As I do hate my wife, my wicked wife!

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: But we may thinke how to recouer all:
If you will act.

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: I will not think; nor act;
Nor yet recouer; do not talke to me?
I'll runne out o' my witts, rather then heare;
I will be what I am, Fabian Fitz-Dottrel,
Though all the world ſay nay to't.

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: Let's follow him.
BVt ha's my Lady miſt me?

sp.speaker: PIT.
sp.l: Beyond telling!
Here ha's been that infinity of ſtrangers!
And then ſhe would ha' had you, to ha' ſampled you
VVith one within, that they are now a teaching;
And do's pretend to your ranck.

sp.speaker: AMB.
sp.l: Good fellow Pit-fall,
Tel Mr. Mere-craft, I intreat a word with him.
Pitfall goes out. This moſt vnlucky accident will goe neare
To be the loſſe o' my place; I am in doubt!

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: VVith me? what ſay you Mr Ambler?

sp.speaker: AMB.
sp.l: Sir,
I would beſeech your worſhip ſtand betweene
Me, and my Ladies diſpleaſure, for my abſence.

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: O, is that all? I warrant you.

sp.speaker: AMB.
sp.l: I would tell you Sir
But how it happened.

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: Briefe, good Maſter Ambler,
Mere-craft ſeemes full of buſineſſe. Put your ſelfe to your rack: for I haue taſque
Of more importance.

sp.speaker: AMB.
sp.l: Sir you'll laugh at me!
But (ſo is Truth) a very friend of mine,
Finding by conference with me, that I liu'd
Too chaſt for my complexion (and indeed
Too honeſt for my place, Sir) did aduiſe me
If I did loue my ſelfe (as that I do,
I muſt confeſſe)

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: Spare your Parentheſis.

sp.speaker: AMB.
sp.l: To gi' my body a little euacuation—

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: Well, and you went to a whore?

sp.speaker: AMB.
sp.l: No, Sr. I durſt not
(For feare it might arriue at ſome body's eare,
Ambler tels this with extraordinary ſpeed. It ſhould not) truſt my ſelfe to a common houſe;
But got the Gentlewoman to goe with me,
And carry her bedding to a Conduit-head,
Hard by the place toward Tyborne, which they call
My L. Majors Banqueting-houſe. Now Sir, This morning
Was Execution; and I ner'e dream't on't,
Till I heard the noiſe o' the people, and the horſes;
And neither I, nor the poore Gentlewoman
Durſt ſtirre, till all was done and paſt: ſo that
I' the Interim, we fell a ſleepe againe. Heflags

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: Nay, if you fall, from your gallop, I am gone Sr.

sp.speaker: AMB.
sp.l: But, when I wak'd, to put on my cloathes, a ſute,
I made new for the action, it was gone,
And all my money, with my purſe, my ſeales,
My hard-wax, and my table-bookes, my ſtudies,
And a fine new deuiſe, I had to carry
My pen, and inke, my ciuet, and my tooth-picks,
All vnder one. But, that which greiu'd me, was
The Gentlewomans ſhoes (with a paire of roſes,
And garters, I had giuen her for the buſineſſe)
So as that made vs ſtay, till it was darke.
For I was faine to lend her mine, and walke
In a rug, by her, barefoote, to Saint Giles'es.

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: A kind of Iriſh penance! Is this all, Sir?

sp.speaker: AMB.
sp.l: To ſatisfie my Lady.

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: I will promiſe you, Sr.

sp.speaker: AMB.
sp.l: I ha' told the true Diſaſter.

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: I cannot ſtay wi' you
Sir, to condole; but gratulate your returne.

sp.speaker: AMB.
sp.l: An honeſt gentleman, but he's neuer at leiſure
To be himſelfe: He ha's ſuch tides of buſineſſe.
O, Call me home againe, deare Chiefe, and put me
To yoaking foxes, milking of Hee-goates,
Pounding of water in a morter, lauing
The ſea dry with a nut-ſhell, gathering all
The leaues are falne this Autumne, drawing farts
Out of dead bodies, making ropes of ſand,
Catching the windes together in a net,
Muſtring of ants, and numbring atomes; all
That hell, and you thought exquiſite torments, rather
Then ſtay me here, a thought more: I would ſooner
Keepe fleas within a circle, and be accomptant
A thouſand yeere, which of 'hem and how far
Out leap'd the other, then endure a minute
Such as I haue within. There is no hell
To a Lady of faſhion. All your tortures there
Ambler comes in, & ſuruayes him Are paſtimes to it. 'T would be a refreſhing
For me, to be i' the fire againe, from hence.

sp.speaker: AMB.
sp.l: This is my ſuite, and thoſe the ſhoes and roſes!

sp.speaker: PVG.
sp.l: Th' haue ſuch impertinent vexations,
Pug perceiues it, and ſtarts. A generall Councell o' diuels could not hit—
Ha! This is hee, I tooke a ſleepe with his Wench,
And borrow'd his cloathes. What might I doe to balke him?

sp.speaker: AMB.
sp.l: Do you heare, Sr?

sp.speaker: PVG.
sp.l: Anſw. him but not to th' purpoſe

sp.speaker: AMB.
sp.l: He anſwers quite from the purpoſe. What is your name, I pray you Sir.

sp.speaker: PVG.
sp.l: Is't ſo late Sir?

sp.speaker: AMB.
sp.l: I aske not o' the time, but of your name, Sir,

sp.speaker: PVG.
sp.l: I thanke you, Sir. Yes it dos hold Sir, certaine.

sp.speaker: AMB.
sp.l: Hold, Sir? What holds? I muſt both hold, and talke to you
About theſe clothes.

sp.speaker: PVG.
sp.l: A very pretty lace!
But the Taylor coſſend me.

sp.speaker: AMB.
sp.l: No, I am coſſend
By you! robb'd.

sp.speaker: PVG.
sp.l: Why, when you pleaſe Sir, I am
For three peny Gleeke, your man

sp.speaker: AMB.
sp.l: Pox o' your gleeke,
And three pence. Giue me an anſwere.

sp.speaker: PVG.
sp.l: Sir,
My maſter is the beſt at it.

sp.speaker: AMB.
sp.l: Your maſter!
Who is your Maſter.

sp.speaker: PVG.
sp.l: Let it be friday night.

sp.speaker: AMB.
sp.l: What ſhould be then?

sp.speaker: PVG.
sp.l: Your beſt ſongs Thom o' Bet'lem

sp.speaker: AMB.
sp.l: I thinke, you are he. Do's he mocke me trow, from purpoſe?
Or do not I ſpeake to him, what I meane?
Good Sir your name.

sp.speaker: PVG.
sp.l: Only a couple a' Cocks Sir,
If we can get a Widgin, 'tis in ſeaſon.

sp.speaker: AMB.
sp.l: For Scepticks. He hopes to make on o' theſe Scipticks o' me
(I thinke I name 'hem right) and do's not fly me.
I wonder at that! 'tis a ſtrange confidence!
I'll prooue another way, to draw his anſwer.
It is the eaſieſt thing Sir, to be done.
As plaine, as ſizzling: roule but wi' your eyes,
And foame at th' mouth. A little caſtle-ſoape
Will do 't, to rub your lips: And then a nutſhell,
With toe, and touch-wood in it to ſpit fire,
Did you ner'e read, Sir, little Darrels tricks,
With the boy o' Burton, and the 7, in Lancaſhire,
Sommers at Nottingham? All theſe do teach it.
And wee'll giue out, Sir, that your wife ha's bewitch'd you:They repaire their old plot

sp.speaker: EVE.
sp.l: And practiſed with thoſe two, as Sorcerers.

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: And ga' you potions, by which meanes you were
Not Compos mentis, when you made your feoffment.
There's no recouery o' your ſtate, but this:
This, Sir, will ſting.

sp.speaker: EVE.
sp.l: And moue in a Court of equity.

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: For, it is more then manifeſt, that this was
A plot o' your wiues, to get your land.

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: I thinke it.

sp.speaker: EVE.
sp.l: Sir it appeares.

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: Nay, and my coſſen has knowne
Theſe gallants in theſe ſhapes.

sp.speaker: EVE.
sp.l: T' haue don ſtrange things, Sir.
One as the Lady, the other as the Squire.

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: How, a mans honeſty may be fool'd! I thought him
A very Lady.

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: So did I: renounce me elſe.

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: But this way, Sir, you'll be reueng'd at height.

sp.speaker: EVE.
sp.l: Vpon 'hem all.

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: Yes faith, and ſince your Wife
Has runne the way of woman thus, e'en giue her—

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: Loſt by this hand, to me; dead to all ioyes
Of her deare Dottrell, I ſhall neuer pitty her:
That could, pitty her ſelfe.

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: Princely reſolu'd Sir,
And like your ſelfe ſtill, in Potentiâ.

div.head: ACT. V. SCENE. IV.
MERE-CRAFT, &c. to them. GVILT-HEAD. SLEDGE. PLVTARCHVS. SERIEANTS.

sp.l: GVilt-head what newes?

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: O Sir, my hundred peices: Fitz-dottrel aſkes for his money.
Let me ha' them yet.

sp.speaker: GVI.
sp.l: Yes Sir, officers
Arreſt him.

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: Me?

sp.speaker: SER.
sp.l: I arreſt you.

sp.speaker: SLE.
sp.l: Keepe the peace,
I charge you gentlemen.

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: Arreſt me? Why?

sp.speaker: GVI.
sp.l: For better ſecurity, Sir. My ſonne Plutarchus
Aſſures me, y' are not worth a groat.

sp.speaker: PLV.
sp.l: Pardon me, Father,
I ſaid his worſhip had no foote of Land left:
And that I'll iuſtifie, for I writ the deed.

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: Ha' you theſe tricks i' the citty?

sp.speaker: GVI.
sp.l: Yes, and more.
Arreſt this gallant too, here, at my ſuite. Meaning Mere-craft

sp.speaker: SLE.
sp.l: I, and at mine. He owes me for his lodging
Two yeere and a quarter.

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: Why M. Guilt-head, Land-Lord,
Thou art not mad, though th' art Conſtable
Puft vp with th' pride of the place? Do you heare, Sirs.
Haue I deſeru'd this from you two? for all
My paines at Court, to get you each a patent

sp.speaker: GVI.
sp.l: The Project of forks For what?

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: Vpo' my proiect o' the forkes,

sp.speaker: SLE.
sp.l: Forkes? what be they?

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: The laudable vſe of forkes,
Brought into cuſtome here, as they are in Italy,
To th' ſparing o' Napkins. That, that ſhould haue made
Your bellowes goe at the forge, as his at the fornace.
I ha' procur'd it, ha' the Signet for it,
Dealt with the Linnen-drapers, on my priuate,
By cauſe, I fear'd, they were the likelyeſt euer
To ſtirre againſt, to croſſe it: for 'twill be
A mighty ſauer of Linnen through the kingdome
(As that is one o' my grounds, and to ſpare waſhing)
Now, on you two, had I layd all the profits.
Guilt-head to haue the making of all thoſe
Of gold and ſiluer, for the better perſonages;
And you, of thoſe of Steele for the common ſort.
And both by Pattent, I had brought you your ſeales in.
Sledge is brought about, And Guilt-head comes. But now you haue preuented me, and I thanke you.

sp.speaker: SLE.
sp.l: Sir, I will bayle you, at mine owne ap-perill.

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: Nay chooſe.

sp.speaker: PLV.
sp.l: Do you ſo too, good Father.

sp.speaker: GVI.
sp.l: I like the faſhion o' the proiect, well,
The forkes! It may be a lucky one! and is not
Intricate, as one would ſay, but fit for
Plaine heads, as ours, to deale in. Do you heare
Officers, we diſcharge you.

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: Why this ſhewes
A little good nature in you, I confeſſe,
But do not tempt your friends thus. Little Guilt-head,
Aduiſe your ſire, great Guilt-head from theſe courſes:
And, here, to trouble a great man in reuerſion,
For a matter o' fifty on a falſe Alarme,
Away, it ſhewes not well. Let him get the pieces
And bring 'hem. Yo'll heare more elſe.
O Maſter Sledge, are you here? I ha' been to ſeeke you.
You are the Conſtable, they ſay. Here's one
That I do charge with Felony, for the ſuite
He weares, Sir.

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: Who? M. Fitz-Dottrels man?
Ware what you do, M. Ambler.

sp.speaker: AMB.
sp.l: Sir, theſe clothes
I'll ſweare, are mine: and the ſhooes the gentlewomans
I told you of: and ha' him afore a Iuſtice,
I will.

sp.speaker: PVG.
sp.l: My maſter, Sir, will paſſe his word for me.

sp.speaker: AMB.
sp.l: O, can you ſpeake to purpoſe now?

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: Not I, Fitz-dottrel diſclaimes him.
If you be ſuch a one Sir, I will leaue you
To your God fathers in Law. Let twelue men worke.

sp.speaker: PVG
sp.l: Do you heare Sir, pray, in priuate.

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: well, what ſay you?
Briefe, for I haue no time to looſe

sp.speaker: PVG.
sp.l: Truth is, Sir,
I am the very Diuell, and had leaue
To take this body, I am in, to ſerue you.
Which was a Cutpurſes, and hang'd this Morning.
And it is likewiſe true, I ſtole this ſuite
To cloth me with. But Sir let me not goe
To priſon for it. I haue hitherto
Loſt time, done nothing; ſhowne, indeed, no part
O' my Diuels nature. Now, I will ſo helpe
Your malice, 'gainſt theſe parties: ſo aduance
The buſineſſe, that you haue in hand of witchcraft,
An your poſſeſſion, as my ſelfe were in you.
Teach you ſuch tricks, to make your belly ſwell,
And your eyes turne, to foame, to ſtare, to gnaſh
Your teeth together, and to beate your ſelfe,
Laugh loud, and faine ſix voices—

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: Out you Rogue!
You moſt infernall counterfeit wretch! Auaunt!
Do you thinke to gull me with your Aeſops Fables?
Here take him to you, I ha' no part in him.

sp.speaker: PVG.
sp.l: Sir.

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: Away, I do diſclaime, I will not heare you. And ſends him away.

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: What ſaid he to you, Sir?

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: Like a lying raskall
Told me he was the Diuel.

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: How! a good reſt!

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: And that he would teach me, ſuch fine diuels tricks
For our new reſolution.

sp.speaker: EVE.
sp.l: O' pox on him,
'Twas excellent wiſely done, Sir, not to truſt him.

sp.speaker: MER
sp.l: Why, if he were the Diuel, we ſha' not need him, Mere-craft giues the inſtructions to him and the reſt.
If you'll be rul'd. Goe throw your ſelfe on a bed, Sir,
And faine you ill. Wee'll not be ſeene wi' you,
Till after, that you haue a fit: and all
Confirm'd within. Keepe you with the two Ladies
And perſwade them. I'll to Iuſtice Either-ſide,
And poſſeſſe him with all. Traines ſhall ſeeke out Ingine,
And they two fill the towne with't, euery cable
Is to be veer'd. We muſt employ out all
Our emiſſaries now; Sir, I will ſend you
Bladders and Bellowes. Sir, be confident,
'Tis no hard thing t' out doe the Deuill in:
A Boy o' thirteene yeere old made him an Aſſe
But t' toher day.

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: Well, I'll beginne to practice,
And ſcape the imputation of being Cuckold,
By mine owne act.

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: yo' are right.

sp.speaker: EVE.
sp.l: Come, you ha' put
Your ſelfe to a ſimple coyle here, and your freinds,
By dealing with new Agents, in new plots.

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: No more o' that, ſweet couſin.

sp.speaker: EVE.
sp.l: What had you
To doe with this ſame Wittipol, for a Lady?

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: Queſtion not that: 'tis done.

sp.speaker: EVE.
sp.l: You had ſome ſtraine
'Boue E-la?

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: I had indeed.

sp.speaker: EVE.
sp.l: And, now, you crack for 't.

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: Do not vpbraid me.

sp.speaker: EVE.
sp.l: Come, you muſt be told on't;
You are ſo couetous, ſtill, to embrace
More then you can, that you looſe all.

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: 'Tis right.
What would you more, then Guilty? Now, your ſuccours.
HEre you are lodg'd, Sir, you muſt ſend your garniſh,
Pug is brought to New gate. If you'll be priuat.

sp.speaker: PVG.
sp.l: There it is, Sir, leaue me.
To New-gate, brought? How is the name of Deuill
Diſcredited in me! What a loſt fiend
Shall I be, on returne? My Cheife will roare
In triumph, now, that I haue beene on earth,
A day, and done no noted thing, but brought
Enter Iniquity the Vice. That body back here, was hang'd out this morning.
Well! would it once were midnight, that I knew
My vtmoſt. I thinke Time be drunke, and ſleepes;
He is ſo ſtill, and moues not! I doe glory
Now i' my torment. Neither can I expect it,
I haue it with my fact.

sp.speaker: INI.
sp.l: Child of hell, be thou merry:
Put a looke on, as round, boy, and red as a cherry.
Caſt care at thy poſternes; and firke i' thy fetters,
They are ornaments, Baby, haue graced thy betters:
Looke vpon me, and hearken. Our Cheife doth ſalute thee,
And leaſt the coldyron ſhould chance to confute thee,
H' hath ſent thee, grant-paroll by me to ſtay longer
A moneth here on earth, againſt cold Child, or honger

sp.speaker: PVG.
sp.l: How? longer here a moneth?

sp.speaker: ING.
sp.l: Yes, boy, till the Seſſion,
That ſo thou mayeſt haue a triumphall egreſſion.

sp.speaker: PVG.
sp.l: In a cart, to be hang'd.

sp.speaker: ING.
sp.l: No, Child, in a Carre,
The charriot of Triumph, which moſt of them are.
And in the meane time, to be greazy, and bouzy,
And naſty, and filthy, and ragged and louzy,
With dam'n me, renounce me, and all the fine phraſes;
That bring, vnto Tiborne, the plentifull gazes.

sp.speaker: PVG.
sp.l: He is a Diuell! and may be our Cheife!
The great Superiour Diuell! for his malice?
Arch-diuel! I acknowledge him. He knew
What I would ſuffer, when he tie'd me vp thus
In a rogues body: and he has (I thanke him)
His tyrannous pleaſure on me, to confine me
To the vnlucky carkaſſe of a Cutpurſe,
Wherein I could do nothing.

sp.speaker: DIV.
sp.l: Impudent fiend, The great Deuill enters, and vpbraids him with all his dayes worke.
Stop thy lewd mouth. Doeſt thou not ſhame and tremble
To lay thine owne dull damn'd defects vpon
An • nnocent caſe, there? Why thou heauy ſlaue!
