The ſpirit, that did poſſeſſe that fleſh before
Put more true life, in a finger, and a thumbe,
Then thou in the whole Maſſe. Yet thou rebell'ſt
And murmur'ſt? What one profer haſt thou made,
Wicked inough, this day, that might be call'd
Worthy thine owne, much leſſe the name that ſent thee?
Firſt, thou did'ſt helpe thy ſelfe into a beating
Promptly, and with 't endangered'ſt too thy tongue:
A Diuell, and could not keepe a body intire
One day! That, for our credit. And to vindicate it,
Hinderd'ſt (for ought thou know'ſt) a deed of darkneſſe:
Which was an act of that egregious folly,
As no one, to'ard the Diuel, could ha' thought on,
This for your acting! but for ſuffering! why
Thou haſt beene cheated on, with a falſe beard,
And a turn'd cloake. Faith, would your predeceſſour
The Cutpurſe, thinke you, ha' been ſo? Out vpon thee,
The hurt th' haſt don, to let men know their ſtrength,
And that the' are able to out-doe a diuel
Put in a body, will for euer be
A ſcarre vpon our Name! whom haſt thou dealt with,
Woman or man, this day, but haue out-gone thee
Some way, and moſt haue prou'd the better fiendes?
Yet, you would be imploy'd? Yes, hell ſhall make you
Prouinciall o' the Cheaters or Bawd-ledger,
For this ſide o' the towne! No doubt you'll render
A rare accompt of things. Bane o' your itch,
And ſcratching for imployment. I'll ha' brimſtone
To al lay it ſure, and fire to ſindge your nayles off,
But, that I would not ſuch a damn'd diſhonor
Sticke on our ſtate, as that the diuell were hang'd;
And could not ſaue a body, Iniquity takes him on his back. that he tooke
From Tyborne, but it muſt come thither againe:
You ſhould e'en ride. But, vp away with him—

sp.speaker: INI.
sp.l: Mount, dearling of darkeneſſe, my ſhoulders are broad:
He that caries the fiend, is ſure of his loade.
The Diuell was wont to carry away the euill;
But, now, the Euill out-carries the Diuell.
A great noiſe is heard in New-gate, and the Keepers come out affrighted. O mee!

sp.speaker: KEE.
sp.l: 1. What's this? 2. A • ece of Iuſtice Hall
Is broken downe. 3. Fough! what eſteeme of brimſtone
Is here? 4. The priſoner's dead, came in but now!

sp.speaker: SHA.
sp.l: Ha? where? 4. Look here.

sp.speaker: KEE.
sp.l: S'lid, I ſhuld know his countenance!
It is Gill-Cut-purſe, was hang'd out, this morning!

sp.speaker: SHA.
sp.l: 'Tis he! 2. The Diuell, ſure, has a hand in this!
3. What ſhall wee doe?

sp.speaker: SHA.
sp.l: Carry the newes of it
Vnto the Sherifes. 1. And to the Iuſtices.
4. This ſtrange! 3. And ſauours of the Diuell, ſtrongly!
2. I' ha' the ſulphure of Hell-coale i' my noſe.
1. Fough.

sp.speaker: SHA.
sp.l: Carry him in. 1. Away. 2. How ranke it is!

div.head: ACT. V. SCENE. VIII.
Sir POVLE. MERE-CRAFT. EVER-ILL. TRAINES. PITFALL. FITZ-DOTTREL. To them VVITTIPOL. MANLY. Miſtreſſe FITZ-DOTTREL. INGINE. To them GVILT-HEAD. SLEDGE. to them SHACKLES.

sp.l: The Iuſtice comes out wandring and the reſt informing him. THis was the notableſt Conſpiracy,
That ere I heard of.

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: Sir, They had giu'n him potions,
That did enamour him on the counterfeit Lady—

sp.speaker: EVE.
sp.l: Iuſt to the time o' deliuery o' the deed—

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: And then the witchcraft 'gan't' appeare, for ſtreight
He fell into his fit.

sp.speaker: EVE.
sp.l: Of rage at firſt, Sir,
Which ſince has ſo increaſed.

sp.speaker: TAY.
sp.l: Good Sr. Poule, ſee him,
And puniſh the impoſtors.

sp.speaker: POV.
sp.l: Therefore I come, Madame.

sp.speaker: EIT.
sp.l: Let Mr Etherſide alone, Madame.

sp.speaker: POV.
sp.l: Do you heare?
Call in the Conſtable, I will haue him by:
H' is the Kings Officer! and ſome Cittizens,
Of credit! I'll diſcharge my conſcience clearly.

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: Yes, Sir, and ſend for his wife.

sp.speaker: EVE.
sp.l: And the two Sorcerers,
By any meanes!

sp.speaker: TAY.
sp.l: I thought one a true Lady,
I ſhould be ſworne. So did you, Eyther-ſide?

sp.speaker: EIT.
sp.l: Yes, by that light, would I might ne'r ſtir elſe, Tailbuſh.

sp.speaker: TAY.
sp.l: And the other a ciuill Gentleman.

sp.speaker: EVE.
sp.l: But, Madame,
You know what I told your Ladyſhip.

sp.speaker: TAY.
sp.l: I now ſee it:
I was prouiding of a banquet for 'hem.
After I had done inſtructing o' the fellow
De-uile, the Gentlemans man

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: Who's found a thiefe, Madam.
And to haue rob'd your Vſher, Maſter Ambler,
This morning.

sp.speaker: TAY.
sp.l: How?

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: I'll tell you more, anon. He beginnes his fit.

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: Gi me ſome garlicke, garlicke, garlicke, garlicke.

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: Harke the poore Gentleman, how he is tormented!

l.hi: My wife is a whore, I'll kiſſe her no more: and why?
Ma'ſt not thou be a Cuckold, as well as I?
Ha, ha, ha, ha, ha, ha, ha, ha, &c.

sp.speaker: POV.
sp.l: That is the Diuell ſpeakes, and laughes in him.

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: Do you thinke ſo, Sr.

sp.speaker: POV.
sp.l: I diſcharge my conſcience. The Iuſtice interpret all:

l.hi: And is not the Diuell good company? Yes, wis.

sp.speaker: EVE.
sp.l: How he changes, Sir, his voyce!

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: Where ere hee put his head, with a a Wanion,

l.hi: If his hornes be forth, the Diuells companion!

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: How he foames!

sp.speaker: EVE.
sp.l: And ſwells!

sp.speaker: TAY.
sp.l: O, me! what's that there, riſes in his belly!

sp.speaker: EIT.
sp.l: A ſtrange thing! hold it downe:

sp.speaker: TRA. PIT.
sp.l: We cannot, Madam.

sp.speaker: POV.
sp.l: 'Tis too apparent this!

sp.speaker: WIT.
sp.l: How now, what play ha' we here.

sp.speaker: MAN.
sp.l: What fine, new matters?Wittipol, and Manly. and Miſtr. Fitz-dottrel enter.

sp.speaker: WIT.
sp.l: The Cockſcomb, and the Couerlet.

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: O ſtrang impudēce!
That theſe ſhould come to face their ſinne!

sp.speaker: EVE:
sp.l: And out-face
Iuſtice, they are the parties, Sir.

sp.speaker: POV.
sp.l: Say nothing.

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: Did you marke, Sir, vpon their comming in,
How he call'd Wittipol.

sp.speaker: EVE.
sp.l: And neuer ſaw 'hem.

sp.speaker: POV.
sp.l: I warrant you did I, let 'hem play a while.

sp.speaker: TAY.
sp.l: Laſſe poore Gentleman!
How he is tortur'd!

sp.speaker: Mrs. FI.
sp.l: Fie, Maſter Fitz-dottrel!
What doe you meane to counterfait thus?

l.hi: Shee comes with a needle, and thruſts it in,
Shee pulls out that, and ſhee puts in a pinne,
And now, and now, I doe not know how, nor where,
But ſhee pricks mee heere, and ſhee pricks me there: ôh, ôh:

sp.speaker: POV.
sp.l: Woman forbeare.

sp.speaker: WIT.
sp.l: What, Sr?

sp.speaker: POV.
sp.l: A practice foule
For one ſo faire:

sp.speaker: WIT.
sp.l: Hath this, then, credit with you?

sp.speaker: MAN.
sp.l: Do you beleeue in't?

sp.speaker: POV.
sp.l: Gentlemen, I'll diſcharge
My conſcience. 'Tis a cleare conſpiracy!
A darke, and diuelliſh practice! I deteſt it!

sp.speaker: WIT.
sp.l: The Iuſtice ſure will proue the merrier man!

sp.speaker: MAN.
sp.l: This is moſt ſtrange, Sir!

sp.speaker: POV.
sp.l: Come not to confront
Authority with impudence: I tell you,
I doe deteſt it. Here comes the Kings Conſtable,
And with him a right worſhipfull Commoner;
My good friend, Maſter Guilt-head! I am glad
I can before ſuch witneſſes, profeſſe
My conſcience, and my deteſtation of it.
Horible! moſt vnaturall! Abominable!

sp.speaker: EVE.
sp.l: They whiſper him. You doe not tumble enough.

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: Wallow, gnaſh:

sp.speaker: TAY.
sp.l: O, how he is vexed!

sp.speaker: POV.
sp.l: 'Tis too manifeſt.

sp.speaker: EVE.
sp.l: Giue him more ſoap to foame with, now lie ſtill.

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: and giue him ſoape to act with. And act a little.

sp.speaker: TAY.
sp.l: What do's he now, Sr.

sp.speaker: POV.
sp.l: Shew
The taking of Tabacco, with which the Diuell
Is ſo delighted.

sp.speaker: POV.
sp.l: And calls for Hum.
You takers of ſtrong Waters, and Tabacco,
Marke this.

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: Yellow, yellow, yellow, yellow, &c.

sp.speaker: POV.
sp.l: That's Starch! the Diuells Idoll of that colour.
He ratifies it, with clapping of his hands.
The proofes are pregnant.

sp.speaker: GVI.
sp.l: How the Diuel can act!

sp.speaker: POV.
sp.l: He is the Maſter of Players! Maſter Guilt-head,
And Poets, too! you heard him talke in rime!
I had forgot to obſerue it to you, ere while!

sp.speaker: TAY.
sp.l: Sir Poule interprets Figgum to be a Iuglers game. See, he ſpits fire.

sp.speaker: POV.
sp.l: O no, he plaies at Figgum,
The Diuell is the Author of wicked Figgum—

sp.speaker: MAN.
sp.l: Why ſpeake you not vnto him?

sp.speaker: WIT.
sp.l: If I had
All innocence of man to be indanger'd,
And he could ſaue, or ruine it: I'ld not breath
A ſyllable in requeſt, to ſuch a foole,
He makes himelfe.

l.hi: Wee ſhall haue more, of Diuells a ſcore,
To come to dinner, in mee the ſinner.

sp.speaker: EYT.
sp.l: Alas, poore Gentleman!

sp.speaker: POV.
sp.l: Put 'hem aſunder.
Keepe 'hem one from the other.

sp.speaker: MAN.
sp.l: Are you phrenticke, Sir,
Or what graue dotage moues you, to take part
VVith ſo much villany? wee are not afraid
Either of law, or triall; let vs be
Examin'd what our ends were, what the meanes?
To worke by; and poſſibility of thoſe meanes.
Doe not conclude againſt vs, ere you heare vs.

sp.speaker: POV.
sp.l: I will not heare you, yet I will conclude
Out of the circumſtances.

sp.speaker: MAN.
sp.l: VVill you ſo, Sir?

sp.speaker: POV.
sp.l: Yes, they are palpable:

sp.speaker: MAN.
sp.l: Not as your folly:

sp.speaker: POV:
sp.l: I will diſcharge my conſcience, and doe all
To the Meridian of Iuſtice:

sp.speaker: GVI.
sp.l: You doe well, Sir.

l.hi: Prouide mee to eat, three or foure diſhes o' good meat,
I'll feaſt them, and their traines, a Iuſtice head and braines

sp.speaker: POV.
sp.l: The Diuell loues not Iuſtice,
There you may ſee.

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: And a whores purt'nance! a Guilt-head whole.

sp.speaker: POV.
sp.l: Be not you troubled, Sir, the Diuell ſpeakes it.

l.hi: Yes, wis, Knight, ſhite, Poule, Ioule, owle, foule, troule, boule.

sp.speaker: POV.
sp.l: Crambe, another of the Diuell's games!

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: Speake, Sir, ſome Greeke, if you can. Is not the Iuſtice
A ſolemne gameſter?

sp.speaker: EVE.
sp.l: Peace.

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: 〈 in non-Latin alphabet 〉 ,
〈 in non-Latin alphabet 〉 ,
〈 in non-Latin alphabet 〉 .

sp.speaker: POV.
sp.l: Hee curſes
In Greeke, I thinke.

sp.speaker: EVE.
sp.l: Your Spaniſh, that I taught you.

sp.speaker: EVE.
sp.l: How? your reſt—
Let's breake his necke in ieſt, the Diuell ſaies,

l.hi: Di grátia, Signòr mioſe haúcte denári fataméne parte.

sp.speaker: MER.
sp.l: What, would the Diuell borrow money?

l.hi: Ouy Monſieur, ùn pàuure Diable! Diablet in!

sp.speaker: POV.
sp.l: It is the diuell, by his ſeuerall languages. Enter the Keeper of New-gate.

sp.speaker: SHA.
sp.l: Where's Sr. Poule Ether-ſide?

sp.speaker: POV.
sp.l: Here, what's the matter?

sp.speaker: SHA.
sp.l: O! ſuch an accident falne out at Newgate, Sir:
A great piece of the priſon is rent downe!
The Diuell has beene there, Sir, in the body—
Of the young Cut-purſe, was hang'd out this morning,
But, in new clothes, Sir, euery one of vs know him.
Theſe things were found in his pocket.

sp.speaker: AMB.
sp.l: Thoſe are mine, Sr.

sp.speaker: SHA.
sp.l: I thinke he was commited on your charge, Sir.
For a new felony

sp.speaker: AMB.
sp.l: Yes.

sp.speaker: SHA.
sp.l: Hee's gone, Sir, now,
And left vs the dead body. But withall, Sir,
Such an infernall ſtincke, and ſteame behinde,
You cannot ſee St. Pulchars Steeple, yet.
They ſmell't as farre as Ware, as the wind lies,
By this time, ſure.

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: Is this vpon your credit, friend? Fitz-dottrel leaues counterfaiting.

sp.speaker: SHA.
sp.l: Sir, you may ſee, and ſatisfie your ſelfe.

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: Nay, then, 'tis time to leaue off counterfeiting.
Sir I am not bewitch'd, nor haue a Diuell:
No more then you. I doe defie him, I,
And did abuſe you. Theſe two Gentlemen
Put me vpon it. (I haue faith againſt him)
They taught me all my tricks. I will tell truth,
And ſhame the Feind. See, here, Sir, are my bellowes,
And my falſe belly, and my Mouſe, and all
That ſhould ha' come forth?

sp.speaker: MAN.
sp.l: Sir, are not you aſham'd
Now of your ſolemne, ſerious vanity?

sp.speaker: POV.
sp.l: I will make honorable amends to truth.

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: And ſo will I. But theſe are Coozeners, ſtill;
And ha' my land, as plotters, with my wife:
Who, though ſhe be not a witch, is worſe, a whore.

sp.speaker: MAN.
sp.l: Sir, you belie her. She is chaſte, and vertuous,
And we are honeſt. I doe know no glory
A man ſhould hope, by venting his owne follyes,
But you'll ſtill be an Aſſe, in ſpight of prouidence.
Pleaſe you goe in, Sir, and heare truths, then iudge 'hem:
And make amends for your late raſhneſſe; when,
You ſhall but heare the paines and care was taken,
To ſaue this foole from ruine (his Grace of Drown'd-land)

sp.speaker: FIT.
sp.l: My land is drown'd indeed—

sp.speaker: POV.
sp.l: Peace.

sp.speaker: MAN.
sp.l: And how much
His modeſt and too worthy wife hath ſuffer'd
By miſ-conſtruction, from him, you will bluſh,
Firſt, for your owne beliefe, more for his actions!
His land is his: and neuer, by my friend,
Or by my ſelfe, meant to another vſe,
But for her ſuccours, who hath equall right.
If any other had worſe counſells in 't,
(I know I ſpeake to thoſe can apprehend mee)
Let 'hem repent 'hem, and be not detected.
It is not manly to take ioy, or pride
In humane errours (wee doe all ill things,
They doe 'hem worſt that loue 'hem, and dwell there,
Till the plague comes) The few that haue the ſeeds
Of goodneſſe left, will ſooner make their way
To a true life, by ſhame, then puniſhment.
